Summary: Error when running models in 32-bit MATLAB on 64-bit Linux
Description: When you run a Simulink model containing Stateflow charts or MATLAB Function blocks in 32-bit MATLAB installed on a 64-bit Linux distribution, the following error is thrown: mex: link of ' "modelName_sfun.mexglx.mexa64"' failed. The indicating factor is the redundant extension "mexa64" in the file name mex  is trying to create. This only occurs in Normal and Accelerator mode.
Workaround: Modify the MEX option file in MATLAB's preferences directory.On the MATLAB Command Line, enter edit([prefdir filesep 'mexopts.sh'])In the file that opens, locate the following two lines: case "$Arch" in     Undetermined)Insert the following line before these two lines: Arch="glnx86"Save mexopts.sh

Summary: Simulink hangs when simulating models in rapid accelerator
Description: Simulink fails at simulating models in Rapid Accelerator mode on recent Red Hat 6.x.  When Simulink models from the play button, the following error is thrown:  Unable to find an unused TCP/IP port to connect to the rapid accelerator target for model 'modelname'   When simulating using the SIM command, MATLAB and the rapid accelerator executable hang. The rapid accelerator executable process needs to be terminated using the kill command from a terminal.

Summary: Possible crash on Windows during Simulink model editing
Description: Simulink may crash during an editing session while it is determining whether a command is undoable. This was fixed by the new editors shipped in R2012b.

Summary: Code inspection using R2012b EB1 results in "out of memory" errors
Description: Simulink Code Inspector R2012b EB1 runs out of memory when inspecting models with nested switching operations.

Summary: Code inspection results in "out of memory" errors
Description: Simulink Code Inspector can run out of memory when inspecting models with data type conversion operations.

Summary: PIL simulation using xmakefilesetup for c2000 targets with ccsv4 or ccv5 configurations (ticcs_c2000_ccsv4 and ticcs_c2000_ccsv5) does not work.
Description: While executing PIL simulation steps described in the help section: "Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL) Simulation for Embedded Targets" for xmakefile setup configurations (ticcs_c2000_ccsv4 and ticcs_c2000_ccsv5), PIL simulation fails for the following reasons: A pause is required to give sufficient time to download the PIL project on the target before running the PIL simulation. The documentation does not mention that this pause is required.
Workaround: A source code fix and a documentation fix are provided below.As described in the help, the download command can be inserted on the "execute" tab of the xmakefilesetup tool.The rtw.connectivity.Launcher.setStartApplicationPause(seconds) can be used to allow a pause after the execution of the download command to guarantee its completion before running the PIL simulation.The PIL Launcher.m method has to honor this pause at the right time. A modified Launcher.m is provided with this bug report.Source Code Fix :1. Rename Launcher.p present in the folder : $(MATLABROOT)\toolbox\idelink\foundation\+linkfoundation\+pil\@Launcher. 2. Copy Launcher.m present in the attached zip file to the foder $(MATLABROOT)\toolbox\idelink\foundation\+linkfoundation\+pil\@Launcher.3. Open MATLAB. Run rehash toolboxcache at the MATLAB command prompt.4. Run the following command at the MATLAB command prompt to set a 30 second delay for the download operation. rtw.connectivity.Launcher.setStartApplicationPause(30)5. Run the PIL simulation for your model configured as described in the MATLAB help.Documentation Fix :The text provided in the documentation section "Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL) Simulation for Embedded Targets > Running Your PIL Application to Perform Simulation and Verification" should be replaced with the following update:After you add your PIL block to your model, using the following command on the MATLAB command prompt, add the required pause needed for download in seconds:rtw.connectivity.Launcher.setStartApplicationPause(30)Click Simulation > Run or press Ctrl+T to run the PIL simulation and view the results.??

Summary: Incorrect Simulink Verification and Validation model coverage test models and expected result files
Description: Some Simulink Verification and Validation model coverage models and expected result files are incorrect. The expected and actual model coverage test results differ for the following model coverage test models: mcase  mdintegrate  mLookupNDas2D   You generate actual test results by executing model coverage test procedures that use the test models. The model coverage test models include DocBlocks  with expected test results. When you execute the model coverage test procedures using the test models, the expected and actual model coverage results should not differ. For information on executing test cases and procedures, see the Simulink Verification and Validation Test Cases, Procedures, and Results  document. The workaround provides the correct model coverage test models and expected result files for the above cases. Additionally, the workaround provides fixes for the following bugs: EBR 956711  EBR 1012251  EBR 576512  EBR 1052010  EBR 1055372

Summary: Occasionally, Simulink Web View may not open properly on Japanese platforms.
Description: Simulink Web View may not open properly on Japanese platforms when there are property values that are longer than 64 characters. This is more likely to happen in annotations in the model, or as part of comments in a Stateflow Chart.
Workaround: Aside from limiting the length of property values and annotations, a suitable workaround may be to use R2012b or later for generating Simulink Web View. If these workarounds are not feasible, please contact MathWorks technical support to investigate other options.

Summary: Compilation macros not saved when using a template
Description: When you use a template that is based on a previous configuration, the software does not save lists of compilation macros. Therefore, the macros that you wanted to export are not specified in your new project.
Workaround: Use one of the following workarounds:In the generated template, manually modify lists for the options -I, -D and -include. For example, replace MACRO1 MACRO2 by [MACRO1,MACRO2].Share the configuration file (.cfg) instead of the template file (.cft).

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated AUTOSAR Version 4.0-compliant C code passes array arguments to RTE functions using incorrect level of indirection
Description: In AUTOSAR Version 4.0-compliant C code, an array argument should be passed to Run-Time Environment (RTE) functions as a pointer to the array base type. However, Embedded Coder incorrectly generates RTE function calls in which an array argument is passed as a pointer to the array type. For example, in R2012a, if you open the example model rtwdemo_autosar_multirunnables , set the AUTOSAR schema version to 4.0, export functions from subsystem ASWC, and inspect the generated file ASWC.c, the generated code for Runnable2  passes array argument rtb_Delay_a  using an incorrect level of indirection, as follows:   int8_T rtb_Delay_a[2];...  Rte_IWrite_Runnable2_Step_PPort_DE3(&rtb_Delay_a);

Summary: Documentation files unavailable with installed version of MATLAB Production Server
Description: The doc component for MATLAB Production Server was not packaged for 12b. Therefore, documentation (Help) files are not installed with the product, in the Program Files\MATLAB Production Server  folder. The documentation for MATLAB Production Server is available on the Web in R2012b. MATLAB Production Server ships without MATLAB and therefore, without a Help browser. There is no built in mechanism for viewing the Help files in the installed product. The Help files must be viewed from the Web. In future releases, documentation files may ship with MATLAB Production Server. For R2012b, however, users must browse the documentation in HTML and PDF format, via the Web.
Workaround: Browse MATLAB Production Server documentation in HTML and PDF format on the Web in the Mathworks Documentation Center.PDF copies of the MATLAB Production Server documentation are attached to this EBR, for convenience.?

Summary: Multi-Winding Transformer block connections become corrupt
Description: Your model contains a subsystem with a Multi-Winding Transformer block. You simulate the model with no errors. If you close, reopen, and simulate the model, the transformer connections are disconnected or incorrectly connected to the rest of the circuit.
Workaround: This issue is due to the fact that the block did not get a chance to fully setup its connections before the simulation start. To avoid this problem you need to open/close once all the subsystems containing the transformer blocks, before starting the simulation for the first time. You only need to open/close subsystems only one time, after the model is loaded.

Summary: TRAIN fails when called within PARFOR or SPMD blocks
Description: Calling TRAIN within a PARFOR or SPMD block fails because NNTRAINTOOL attempts to open a Java window and Java is not available within parallel blocks.
Workaround: The attached file nntraintool.m can be put in the workspace folder or used to replace the installed version of toobox/nnet/nnguis/nntraintool.m.

Summary: Some models fail to build when "Optimize using specified minimum and maximum values" is enabled
Description: Some models fail to build when the "Optimize using specified minimum and maximum values" model configuration parameter is enabled. When this happens, the following error message occurs: CGIR assertion 'cg_node_check_enum(aExpr) == CG_CHECK_MODELCOV' failed in '.\dom_core\analysis\range\LocalAnalysisImpl.cpp:1530'
Workaround: From the model menu, select Simulation > Model Configuration Parameters.On the Configuration Parameters dialog box Code Generation pane, disable the "Optimize using specified minimum and maximum values" parameter.

Summary: isnan, isinf, isfinite functions used with System objects cause code generation assertion
Description: Generating code for MATLAB code that uses isnan , isinf  or isfinite  functions with System objects may cause an assertion to occur.
Workaround: If you need to call isnan, isinf, or isfinite on a vector use a for loop.For example, change the codeTF = isnan(angle);    % angle is a vectortoTF = false(size(angle));for ii=1:numel(angle)  TF(ii) = isnan(angle(ii));endIf this impacts simulation speed to an excessive degree, you can use coder.target to create a workaround only for code generation. For example,if isempty(coder.target) % Only runs in MATLAB TF = isnan(angle);else % Only runs in codegen TF = false(size(angle)); for ii=1:numel(angle)    TF(ii) = isnan(angle(ii)); end end

Summary: Error when generating code for model using data store memory and buses with alias data types
Description: An error may be reported when generating code for a model that meets all  of the following conditions:  The model has a data store memory (local or global) that is configured to use a bus signal. The Data Store Read and Data Store Write blocks associated with such a data store memory operate on an element in this bus (calling get_param(block, 'DataStoreElements')  does not return an empty string). The data type associated with such a bus element is an alias data type.  The error is of the form: CGIR assertion 'isStruct() && isScalar()' failed in 'dom_core/analysis/range/../../../export/include/cgir_xform/analysis/range/RangeValue.hpp:107'
Workaround: Consider using one of these workarounds:Change the data type of the bus element that is being operated on to use the underlying base type instead of an alias type. Change the Data Store Read and Data Store Write blocks to operate on the entire bus signal, instead of operating on selective elements.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Inconsistent out-of-range handling for input signal of Switch Case in simulation and code generation
Description: The Switch Case block converts its input signal value to 32-bit signed integer. As a result, this conversion requires out-of-range handling for input signals that differ from the integer type. The handler mechanism used in simulation and generated code are inconsistent in the following ways causing a difference in simulation and code generation results: Case 1:  The input signal has an integer data type that causes an overflow (e.g. uint32 )In simulation, the conversion saturates on overflow, while in the generated code, the conversion wraps on overflow. Case 2:  The input signal is an floating point data type (e.g. double  or single )In simulation, the conversion saturates on integer overflow and uses Zero  integer rounding mode. In the generated code, the conversion wraps on integer overflow and uses Simplest  integer rounding mode. In addition, the generated code does not honor the Configuration Parameters  > Optimization > Remove code from floating-point to integer conversions that wraps out-of-range values  parameter. Specifically, the code that wraps out-of-range values for floating-point to integer conversion is always  removed. This eliminates the possibility of ensuring that the simulation and code generation results match.
Workaround: To work around this issue, design your system to ensure that for block inputs of integer data type, the input signal remains within the non-negative range of 32-bit signed integer. It is preferable to avoid double or single precision input signal values to these two blocks and use integer types instead, to achieve clearer modeling semantics and simple generated code.

Summary: Linearizing models with Data Store Read or Data Store Write blocks can result in invalid error
Description: Linearizing models containing Data Store Read or Data Store Write blocks that fully access the associated data store memory, which specifies its Data Type  as a Simulink.AliasType , can result in an invalid error in this form:  Linearization is not supported for models that contain Data Store Write blocks like 'XYZ', that have more than one input port or access only a selected few elements in the associated Data Store Memory 'DSM'.
Workaround: Instead of using the Simulink.AliasType directly to specify the data type on the Data Store Memory block (local) or the Simulink.Signal object (global), use the underlying BaseType associated with this Simulink.AliasType. For example, if you have a Simulink.Signal object myGlobalDSM to define a global data store memory, and if this memory uses an alias type myAliasToDouble to specify its DataType, then you can work around this issue by directly using the underlying base type to define the data store memory: Cache the original AliasType originalAliasType = myGlobalDSM.DataType. Apply the workaround by using BaseType myGlobalDSM.DataType = myAliasToDouble.BaseType.Call any linearization function; for example, linearize(model). Revert back changes to myGlobalDSM.DataType = originalAliasType.

Summary: Annotations ignored when using -add-to-results-repository
Description: If you specify the option -add-to-results-repository  and run a server verification, code annotations are not preserved in the results repository for Polyspace Metrics. Consider the following code. extern int fStub(void);int fPlusOne(void) { int l_x; /* polyspace<RTE:OVFL:Not a defect:No action planned> due to fStub() */ l_x = fStub()+1; return l_x; }  When the verification is complete, the justification for the OVFL check is not preserved in the results repository and is not reflected in the review values of Polyspace Metrics.
Workaround: Use one of the following workarounds:Manual workaround:Run a Polyspace server verification without specifying the option -add-to-results-repository.In the Polyspace verification environment, use the Results Manager to view the results.On the Results Manager toolbar, click the Open Polyspace Metrics web interface button, which uploads verification results to the results repository.Install one of the attachments on a Polyspace server R2012a:Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your Polyspace installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your Polyspace installation. By default this is in c:\Polyspace or in /usr/local/Polyspace on Linux systems. This folder is referred to as <POLYSPACE> in subsequent steps. Polyspace_Common and PolyspaceFor<lang>_R2012a are the two principal sub-folders of <POLYSPACE>.Stop Polyspace queue manager service.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell:unzip -l <POLYSPACE>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <POLYSPACE> folder.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell: cd <POLYSPACE>unzip <.zip file>Check that binaries have appropriate ¡®read & execute¡¯ permissions.Delete the .zip file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Restart Polyspace queue manager service

Summary: Links do not work after enabling Embedded Coder support for opened results
Description: Suppose you perform the following steps: From your Simulink model window, select ... Polyspace > Open Results > For Generated Code , which opens the Results Manager in the Polyspace verification environment. In the Results Manager perspective, select Review > Code Generator Support > Embedded Coder .  The return-to-model links in the Results Manager perspective do not work. For example, clicking visible links in the Source  view does not take you back to the Simulink model.
Workaround: For a workaround, do one of the following:Manually add a MATLABROOT environment variable in the file code_generator_used:Close the Polyspace verification environment.In the results folder, use a text editor to open the code_generator_used file.Add the environment variable MATLABROOT, which specifies the path to the version of MATLAB being used. For example, if MATLAB R2012a is located in C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2012a, then insert the following on a new line at the end of the file: MATLABROOT=C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2012aSave the file. Then reopen your results.Install the attachment found in bug report 849362.

Summary: Generating code for a model containing virtual bus inputs to model reference variant blocks may result in an invalid error
Description: An invalid error may be thrown if all  of the following conditions exist:  Code is being generated for this model. This model contains a model reference variant block with one or more virtual bus inputs. These virtual buses are constructed without associating them with bus objects.
Workaround: If the input into the model reference variant port is a virtual bus signal, insert a Signal Conversion block in front of this port and configure the inserted block with the following options: Set Output to Nonvirtual bus.Set Data Type as the same bus object as that used in the corresponding Inport block of the referenced model.

Summary: NonLinearModel.fit generates the error ()-indexing must appear last in an index expression
Description: If you fit a nonlinear regression model using NonLinearModel.fit , and specify the model using a formula string that includes a coefficient named b  or a predictor variable named X , the function might generate the following error: >> load carbig>> fit = NonLinearModel.fit(Weight,MPG,'MPG ~ a + b/Weight',[1 1],'CoefficientNames',{'a' 'b'})Error using classreg.regr.NonLinearFormula/get.ModelFun (line 114)Error: ()-indexing must appear last in an index expression.Error in NonLinearModel/fitter (line 1085)            F = model.Formula.ModelFun;Error in classreg.regr.FitObject/doFit (line 219)            model = fitter(model);Error in NonLinearModel.fit (line 1484)            model = doFit(model);
Workaround: If you use the 'CoefficientNames' name-value pair argument, list the coefficient named b first. If you use the 'VarNames' name-value pair argument, list the predictor variable named X first. For example,fit = NonLinearModel.fit(Weight,MPG,'MPG ~ a + b/Weight',[1 1],'CoefficientNames',{'b' 'a'})

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: From File block uses different interpolation for Normal mode and non-host-based code generation modes
Description: Generating code for From File block in GRT or other non-host-based simulation mode always uses linear interpolation, regardless of the interpolation specified on the block. This causes the From File block to produce different results for Normal mode and non-host-based code generation modes.

Description: In Normal Mode, the CAN message identifier parameter is not processed correctly. As a result, CAN messages with certain message identifiers are not being sent or received correctly.

Summary: Accidentally dragging an entry-point file in a MATLAB Coder project clears the input type definitions for the file
Description: If you accidentally drag an entry-point file slightly (for example, while trying to open it) and drop it within the entry-point file section, then any input type definitions that you have entered for that file are lost. This happens because the software treats this action the same as if you removed and re-added the file to the project.
Workaround: Follow these steps to install a patch that eliminates the issue.In MATLAB, run the matlabroot command to determine where to install the patch.Download the appropriate file for your version of MATLAB, and save it in the location indicated in Step 1. For R2011a and R2011b, download patch-g848226-11.zip. For R2012a, download patch-g848226-12.zip.In MATLAB, run the command: cd(matlabroot)Use the MATLAB unzip command to unzip the file you downloaded in Step 2. For example: unzip('patch-g848226-11.zip')Restart MATLAB. This will apply the patch.

Summary: polybool workaround for bufferm issue does not always work as intended
Description: The R2010b Mapping Toolbox Release Notes include the following: Change in Behavior for bufferm  If you specify 'out'  for the direction  string when calling the bufferm  function, the returned buffer zone will include all points outside the polygon within a specified distance of its edge. In previous releases, the returned buffer zone also included the points within the polygon.  Compatibility Considerations  If you have code that specifies 'out'  for the direction string, you now receive only the region outside the polygon but not the polygon itself. If you want to receive the union of the polygon and the buffer zone, as you did in previous releases, use the polybool  function.  In some cases, this workaround fails because of numerical round-off issues. Small fragments of the input polygon can appear within the interior of the output.

Summary: In R2012a PUBLISH may take very long to complete
Description: Under rare licensing situations, the VER command can take a long time to complete in R2012a. Publishing uses the VER command to get the current release info, so it can be affected.
Workaround: Change line 52-53 of matlab\toolbox\matlab\codetools\private\m2mxdom.m frommatlabVersion = ver('MATLAB');newTextNode = dom.createTextNode(matlabVersion.Version);to matlabVersion = '7.14';newTextNode = dom.createTextNode(matlabVersion);

Summary: Overclocking information for FIL I/O is incorrect.
Description: The overclocking information for FIL I/O is incorrect. The correct formula is as follows.For FILSimulation System object: Total input must be less than 1467 bytesWhere total input data set equals the sum of the input size rounded up to bytes Output data set must also be less than 1467 bytesWhere total output data set equals the sum of the output size rounded up to bytes * overclocking factor  For FIL Simulation block: Total output must be less than 1467 bytesWhere total input data set equals the sum of the input size rounded up to bytes Output data set must also be less than 1467 bytesWhere total output data set equals the sum of the output size rounded up to bytes * overclocking factor  For example, a subsystem with one 9-bit input (2 bytes) and one 16-bit input (2 bytes) would have an input data set of 4 bytes.The overclocking factor is calculated based on one of these equations:OCF = fastest_dut_sample_rate/fastest_input_sample_rateOrOCF = input_sample_time / fastest_dut_sample_timeWhere sample_time = 1/sample_rate.The overclocking factor is greater than 1 when there is upsampling in the DUT, which results in a smaller DUT sample time compared to the input.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Constant sample time triggered subsystems might generate incorrect code
Description: A subsystem might generate incorrect code if all of the following are true: The subsystem is a triggered or an enabled and triggered subsystem.  The subsystem has a constant sample time. At least one of the output ports is connected to a signal with a testpoint, or a signal with a non-Auto  signal storage class.
Workaround: A constant sample time triggered subsystem is never triggered. Therefore, one possible workaround is to remove this subsystem.

Summary: Generating code for model with bus loop may crash Simulink
Description: Simulink crashes when generating code from model when all  of the following conditions exist: The Bus Assignment block that loads the bus signal is part of a feedback loop. This block operates on a non-virtual bus. The bus object and the signals in the model have different element names.
Workaround: Break up the bus loop by:Make sure that the names of signals and the fields in a bus object match each other.In the bus loop, add a Signal Specification block that specifies the bus object.

Summary: HDA read functions might return incorrect data or errors for certain time stamps
Description: When requesting HDA data from an OPC server using readAtTime , readProcessed , readRaw , or readModified  functions, you might receive data outside of the times requested, or the operation might fail. This happens only for certain time stamps. For example, requesting raw data from the Matrikon OPC Simulation Server starting from April 4 2012 at 14:05 returns an error: >> hdaObj.readRaw('Random.Real8', '2012-04-04 14:05', '2012-04-04 14:07')Error using opc.hda.Client/readRaw (line 413)Invalid argument error returned from client thread. However, requesting data from 14:04 returns the correct result: >> hdaObj.readRaw('Random.Real8', '2012-04-04 14:04', '2012-04-04 14:07')Warning: Random.Real8: No data was found in the specified time range. > In Client>Client.processReadErrors at 951 In Client>Client.readRaw at 419 ans = 1-by-1 OPC HDA Data object: ItemID       Value   Start TimeStamp End TimeStamp Quality ------------ ------- --------------- ------------- ------- Random.Real8 <empty>  --             --            <empty>
Workaround: A patch is available for MATLAB R2011b and R2012a. Users with a release of MATLAB earlier than R2011b must upgrade to a later release to take advantage of this patch.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder. Be sure to choose the appropriate .zip file for your MATLAB release. You can find out which release of MATLAB you have installed from the splash screen, or in the MATLAB desktop click Help > About MATLAB.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file, open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the .zip file to your <MATLAB> folder.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: "Simulink Design Verifier failed to initialize: Checking compatibility failed" error for models containing dynamic lookup tables with fixed point data types
Description: Simulink Design Verifier gives the following error message for models containing Lookup Table Dynamic  blocks with signals of fixed point data type: Simulink Design Verifier failed to initialize: Checking compatibility failed

Summary: Wind-Turbine Asynchronous Generator in Isolated Network example (power_windgen model) does not simulate because of wrong initialization of inital state vector
Description: When you try to run the power_windgen model you get an error message similar to the following:  Model error - Initial state vector "X0" must be a real vector of length 95
Workaround: There are two possible workarounds: A. Clear the Initial state check box on the Data Import/Export pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box. The model will simulate but will not start exactly from the initial conditions that are expected in the demonstration. B. Regenerate the initial state vector, using the following procedure: 1. Open power_windgen model. 2. Set the stop time to 10 seconds. 3. Open the 3-Phase Breaker block and set the Transition times parameter to 1e6.4. Run the simulation. 5. When the simulation is terminated, type the following commands at the matlab prompt: xInitial = xFinal; save power_windgen.mat xInitial 6. Close power_windgen without saving the model.

Summary: Release notes in R2012a do not mention the change in real and imag
Description: The following change was not announced in the release notes for R2012a. In release R2011b and earlier, real  and imag  are defined via the conj  function: syms zreal(z)imag(z) ans =z/2 + conj(z)/2 ans =- (z*i)/2 + (conj(z)*i)/2 Starting from the release R2012a, real  and imag  are no longer defined via conj . They use the MuPAD Re  and Im  functions instead. Therefore, real  and imag  can return results in a different form. Results returned by real  and imag  now are mathematically equivalent to the results returned in previous releases.

Summary: Discrete Integrator block not have the needSatDwork in code gen when limits are outside the output datatype range
Description: Discrete Integrator block has an optimization to saturate the output on upper/lower limits. The generated code will be different when the optimization is on/off. When the limit values are outside the range of the output datatype, whether the saturation is turned on or off does not affect the numeric results, because the output will not exceed the limits at all. However the generated code should still have a needSatDwork to show the optimization is on.

Summary: Incorrect green IDP check after decrement of pointer to scalar variable
Description: Consider the following code: void foo(void) { int x; int *p = &x; p--; *p = 1; /* green IDP that should be red */} In a verification, the software might generate an incorrect green IDP check for *p . This problem can occur only with scalars and does not affect structures or arrays. ?

Summary: Linker or loader error in SIL or PIL simulation when function execution time profiling is enabled and referenced model rebuild option is set to If any changes in known dependencies detected
Description: You might see a linker or loader error when you perform the following set of steps: Create a top model that contains a Model block.  Configure the top model to simulate in Normal mode and the Model block to simulate in SIL or PIL mode. For the top model, set Configuration Parameters > Model Referencing > Rebuild  to If any changes in known dependencies detected . You can perform this action from the command line by setting the parameter UpdateModelReferenceTargets  to 'IfOutOfDate' . For the top model, select the Configuration Parameters > SIL and PIL Verification > Measure task execution time  check box. For the referenced model, select the Configuration Parameters > SIL and PIL Verification > Measure function execution times  check box. Simulate the top model. Repeat the simulation. On this second run, you might see a linker error, for example, undefined symbol or reference .  The exact error message is compiler dependent. If you run a PIL simulation on a platform that supports dynamic linking of shared libraries, the error might occur only when the compiled code is downloaded to the target hardware.
Workaround: Try one of the following:Change the Rebuild option to If any changes detected , that is, set the parameter UpdateModelReferenceTargets to 'IfOutOfDateOrStructuralChange'.Disable function execution profiling for the referenced model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Multirate model that contains a function call to a Model block
Description: A multirate model that contains a function call to a Model block might generate incorrect code. The code generation process might delete the function body for either the output or subrate step function. This incorrect code generation behavior might happen sporadically.
Workaround: Place the Model block called by the multirate model into a subsystem.

Summary: readImg errors on FITS images with certain scaling parameters
Description: The readImg  function in the matlab.io.fits  package errors if used on a uint8 , int16 , or int32  image that scales to an int8 , uint16 , or uint32  data type. For example, suppose you have an int16  FITS image written as follows: HDU:  1 (Primary HDU)        SIMPLE  =                    T / file does conform to FITS standard        BITPIX  =                   16 / number of bits per data pixel        NAXIS   =                    2 / number of data axes        NAXIS1  =                   16 / length of data axis 1        NAXIS2  =                   16 / length of data axis 2        BZERO   =               32768. / offset data range to that of unsigned short        BSCALE  =                   1. / default scaling factor The BZERO   and BSCALE   values as specified scale any int16  pixel value so that the CFITSIO library can represent it as a uint16  value. The readImg  function returns an error if used on this image.

Summary: Memory leak while generating continuously on analog subsystems
Description: When you generate analog output continuously using the session-based interface, MATLAB becomes slow to respond and will eventually stop responding.

Summary: lassoglm errors with MCreps values other than 1.
Description: Using lassoglm  with both the CV  (cross-validation) option and MCReps  with a value other than 1 results in the following error: Error using *  Inner matrix dimensions must agree.  When you use cross-validation in lassoglm , a value of "deviance" is reported for each fold. lassoglm  then adjusts the deviance values, scaling them from the size of the test set for each fold to the size of the full input data set. In performing this adjustment, the code uses the number of folds but fails to recognize that the number of deviance values returned is #folds*#mcreps . This results in a dimension mismatch for element-by-element multiplication.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Shift Arithmetic block might generate incorrect code for complex inputs
Description: Shift Arithmetic block generates incorrect code when ALL of the following conditions are true:  Block input data type is complex integer or fixed point.  Bits to shift > Source  is set to "Input port". Bits to shift  value is greater than the word length of the input signal, i.e. it is out-of-range.  Check for out-of-range 'Bits to shift' in generated code  check box is selected.  ?
Workaround: To work around this issue, follow these steps:Separate the real and imaginary parts of the complex input signal. Connect the input signal to a Complex to Real-Imag block.On the real and imaginary parts of the input signal, perform the bit shift you want. Connect each output of the Complex to Real-Imag block to a Shift Arithmetic block.Reform the complex signal. Connect the outputs of the two Shift Arithmetic blocks to a Real-Imag to Complex block.

Summary: Incorrect results for departure, and for  'parallel' option in rcurve function
Description: Except in the case of a sphere (or for points on the equator or at a pole), the values returned when using the 'parallel'  option with the rcurve  function are incorrect. This bug directly affects the results of the departure  function, as well.  It also affects the results of the stdm  function, but only very slightly.
Workaround: Replace calls like:rcurve(ellipsoid,lat,angleUnits)rcurve('parallel',ellipsoid,lat,angleUnits)with:rcurve('transverse',ellipsoid,lat,angleUnits) * cos(toRadians(angleUnits,lat)) Replace calls like:departure(lon1,lon2,lat,ellipsoid,angleUnits)with:N = rcurve('transverse',ellipsoid,lat,angleUnits);divisor = fromDegrees(angleUnits,360);dlon = lon2 - lon1;dlon = min(mod(dlon,divisor), mod(-dlon,divisor));toRadians(angleUnits,dlon) .* N .* cos(toRadians(angleUnits,lat))

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Configuring models with a single periodic rate and one or more asynchronous rates for concurrent execution might generate incorrect code
Description: Configuring models with a single periodic rate and one or more asynchronous rates for concurrent execution causes the task for the mapped periodic rate to not run for multicore systems that use target platforms supported with Embedded Coder. A model that is affected by this issue must meet all  of the following conditions: Is configured for Embedded Coder targets (ert.tlc  with cleared Classic call interface  check box or idelink_ert.tlc ). Has one or more asynchronous rates. Has a single periodic rate. Has no asynchronous block that needs absolute time. If an asynchronous block needs absolute time, it manages its own absolute time.
Workaround: As a workaround, configure for embedded targets as follows, depending on your system target file:For system target files set to ert.tlc, in the Configuration Parmaeters dialog box, select the Interface > Classic call interface check box.For system target files set to idelink_ert.tlc, in the Configuration Parmaeters dialog box, change System target file to idelink_grt.tlc.

Summary: Error message nonstandard form for taking the address of a member function during compilation
Description: With certain code, Polyspace may generate the following error message during the compilation phase of a C++ verification: file.c, line xxx: error: nonstandard form for taking the address of a member function This error is associated with the use of the options -OS-target visual  and -dialect visual9.0 . As this kind of error is code-specific, it is not possible to give a code example that reproduces the problem.
Workaround: If you see this message, rerun the verification using the option: -cppfe-extra-flags -diag_warning=504This action should resolve the problem, and has no impact on your results.From the command line, you can use this option with any other option. Note that there are two parts to the option, that is, -cppfe-extra-flags must be followed by -diag_warning=504.You can also enable the option through the Polyspace verification environment:In the Project Manager perspective, on the Configuration pane, expand the Analysis Option > Polyspace inner settings node.In the Other options field, enter -cppfe-extra-flags -diag_warning=504.

Summary: Setting UTM zone from within axesmui GUI causes error
Description: An error results when setting the UTM zone from within the axesmui  GUI. The error message says:Undefined function or variable "txtmsg".  The error arises when you press the Zone button, select a zone interactively on the map, and press the Accept button. The same error also arises if you simply edit the zone field in the Projection Control window and press the Apply button.
Workaround: You can set the UTM zone with setm instead of using the GUI.

Instead of 'InputSignals', '', ... For a sink: 'OutputSignals', , ... Instead of 'OutputSignals', '' , ... ?

Summary: Documentation on dead logic detection incorrectly includes decision outcomes for Saturate on integer overflow parameter
Description: In the R2012a Simulink Design Verifier documentation, the "Detecting Dead Logic" section states that when the Saturate on integer overflow  parameter is selected, model objects receive dead logic detection on decision outcomes for overflow saturation. This is incorrect; model objects do not receive dead logic detection for overflow saturation decision logic, regardless of the Saturate on integer overflow  parameter.

Summary: Creating Stateflow data with empty names sometimes causes MATLAB to crash
Description: When you add data to a Stateflow chart in the Model Explorer, the data is sometimes created with empty names. If you then set the scope of unnamed data to chart input or output, MATLAB might crash when you update the diagram. The cause of the problem is a faulty file called sfdefaults , which is saved in the MATLAB preferences directory. You create the sfdefaults  file when you save Stateflow style preferences to disk by: Selecting Edit > Style  in the Stateflow editor. Selecting Options > Save defaults to disk  in the style editor.
Workaround: You can work around this issue either by preventing empty data from being created or by fixing existing models.To prevent empty data from being created:Delete the faulty sfdefaults file from your preferences directory. To find the full path to the preferences directory, type the following MATLAB command:>> prefdirDeleting this file also clears any style settings for Stateflow. To save your style settings for R2011b and R2012a, apply a patch as explained below.Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached file attachment_829991_11b_12a_2012-05-14.zip and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):  cd <MATLAB> unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.When running on Windows Systems, if you get 'Error: Access is denied' when attempting to extract the .zip file: you might need toStart WinZip in administrator mode by right-clicking WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.To fix existing models:Deleting the sfdefaults file or applying the patch does not fix models that already contain data objects with empty names. If you already have models with empty inputs or outputs, remove those data using the attached function, removeEmptyNamedData. To use this script, unzip the attachment removeEmptyNamedData.zip and then move the file removeEmptyNamedData.m somewhere on the MATLAB path. Then, load an existing model and issue the command:>> removeEmptyNamedData('modelName')If the model contains data objects with empty names, the script:Removes those data objectsIssues a warning on the command line that the model had data objects with empty names.Marks the model as "modified". You can then save the model if you wish to prevent problems in the future. It is also easy to modify the script to save the model instead of just marking it modified. Note that this script only works in MATLAB versions R2011b and R2012a where this bug exists.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Incorrect results when switching between SIL and PIL simulations with referenced models and option Enable portable word sizes
Description: Consider a model that: Uses referenced models Has the option Enable portable word sizes  selected  This bug would affect you if you are doing a top model SIL/PIL simulation and/or a model block SIL/PIL simulation. If you switch between SIL and PIL simulations, the code generation process might use a stale model reference library and produce incorrect results. For example, suppose you perform the following steps: Run a SIL simulation. Run a PIL simulation. Make a change to one of the referenced models, which should force new code to be generated. Run a SIL simulation. Run a PIL simulation.  In step 5, the software does not recompile the newly generated model reference code. Instead, the software uses the model reference library from step 2.
Workaround: Manually remove the stale model reference library for the referenced model that is modified. The model reference library is located in slprj/target/reference_model (relative to the code generation folder). Another workaround is to do the SIL or PIL simulation in a new empty folder.

Summary: NVIDIA Kepler-based GPU cards can return a silent wrong answer when working with large data sets
Description: The NVIDIA Kepler based GPUs (GTX 670, GTX 680, GTX 690, etc.) are not fully qualified for use with MATLAB R2012a. Silent wrong answers are possible when running MATLAB built-in functions on the Kepler family of GPU cards. The silent wrong answer can occur when performing computations with:  The latest NVIDIA driver, and Data sets larger than 65535 elements in any single dimension.  For example, running the the following code with NVIDIA CUDA driver version 4.2 in MATLAB R2012a generates an incorrect result: g = parallel.gpu.GPUArray.ones(16, 70000); s = sum(g, 1); assert(all(gather(s)==16)) The expected result is 16 in all elements of the output vector. However, a Kepler-based card returns a vector containing zeros.
Workaround: To work around this issue in MATLAB R2012a: Download and install the latest version of NVIDIA CUDA drivers: For Windows, use any driver released after July 15, 2012, or the driver that is available with CUDA Toolkit 5.0 RC1. For Linux, download CUDA driver version 304.37 or later. For MAC OS X, download CUDA driver version 5.0.24 or later. After installing the drivers, set the CUDA_GRID_SIZE_COMPAT environment variable to 1 with either of the following methods:Modify the system environment before starting MATLAB. Enter setenv('CUDA_GRID_SIZE_COMPAT','1') at the MATLAB command prompt before issuing any GPU commands.

Summary: For integer and fixed point portions of a model, deriving minimums and maximums can be incorrect or fail if the embedded hardware is not equivalent to a 32-bit target
Description: Fixed-Point Tool can collect derived minimums and maximums for a model. Depending on the embedded hardware that a model is targeting, a bug may cause collection of derived minimums and maximums to be incorrect or fail. The main impact of this bug is to change data types used in the model. This bug may also cause a slight change of rounding behavior for fixed point casts if several conditions are met.1  The main impact of this bug does not affect models configured to target embedded hardware that provides char, short, int and long of 8, 16, 32 and 32 bits. If the model is exposed to this bug, collection of derived ranges may fail or return incorrect results. Floating point and boolean portions of a model are not exposed to this bug. If data type override has been used to replace all integer and fixed point types with double, then this bug is highly unlikely to affect the collection of derived minimums and maximums. You may use the attached script "detectModelExposure" to determine if a model is exposed to this bug. A model's embedded hardware configuration affects the model's behavior. The embedded hardware affects the automatic selection of data types for operations that involve integer or fixed point arguments. The properties of the embedded hardware are used to guide the selection of data types that will lead to a balance of the most desirable mathematical behavior and the most efficient embedded implementation. This bug in the collection of derived minimums and maximums temporarily alters the model's embedded hardware to a generic 32-bit embedded processor. If the model's original embedded hardware was ASIC/FPGA or if the original number of bits for char, short, int or long did not equal 8, 16, 32 and 32, then automatic selection of data types for the original model and the altered model can be different. A change in data types could cause a data type propagation error which would cause collection of derived minimums and maximums to fail. The change of data type can also lead to different mathematical behavior between the original model and the altered model. Altering the mathematical behavior of the model can produce derived minimums and maximums that are incorrect. Included in the workaround attachments are two models that demonstrate when this bug can be triggered. In the first model, "mSimulinkExample" the sum block is configured to use internal rules to determine the accumulator and output data types. In the second model "mStateflowExample", a relational operator in the Stateflow action language is used with mixed integer types. In both models, the embedded hardware configuration affects automatic data type selection and resulting mathematical behavior. Due to this bug of temporarily altering the embedded hardware, the derived minimums and maximums collected for these models are incorrect. Note 1 : Independent of the main issue of changing data types, this bug may also cause a slight change of rounding behavior for fixed point casts if all of the following criteria are true. The original embedded hardware specified that signed integer division rounds to Zero or Floor. The optimization 'Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers' is selected. The model uses fixed point types with Slopes that are not powers of two.  When all these criteria are true, some fixed-point casts may have a 1 bit rounding difference between the original model and the altered model.

Summary: Incorrect simulation results in closed loops with Gimbal, Bearing, or Bushing Joint blocks
Description: A bug in Gimbal, Bearing, and Bushing Joint blocks can cause the model to simulate incorrectly. The bug affects only closed loops that contain one or more affected Joint blocks. No warning or error message warns user that simulation problem exists. Model simulation proceeds as usual, with no explicit indication of a simulation problem.  The bug does not affect open loop systems.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: SampleTime value input by user during MATLAB System object generation with the cosimWizard is ignored.
Description: When generating a MATLAB cosim System object, the last step of the cosimWizard asks the user to specify a different value for the SampleTime, if desired. However, the cosimWizard ignores any value input by the user and instead uses the SampleTime default value when it generates the System object code.

Summary: graycomatrix does not have equal width bins for the initial quantization mapping that is applied to the input image.
Description: As an initial step, graycomatrix  maps the intensity range of the input image to the number of levels specified by the parameter 'NumLevels' (default 8). This step works by dividing the input image's intensity range into bins, and mapping each bin to a unique level in the mapped image. In the mapping for versions up through R2012a, the widths of the first and the last bins are smaller than the rest of the bins. This reduces the probability of a pixel falling in these two bins as compared to the other bins. The new R2012b version of graycomatrix  uses a mapping where all the bins are equal width and equally spaced. This equalizes the probability for all the bins and leads to a uniformly quantized mapped image.
Workaround: To work around this problem:1) Log into your computer using an account with Administrator privileges.2) If you are using a R2010b, R2011a or R2011b version, download the attached file, workaround_828134_10b_through_11b_2012-06-20.zip. Or, if you are using R2012a, download the attached file, workaround_828134_12a_2012-06-20.zip3) On Windows double-click the file to view its contents, then click the Extract button. On Macintosh, double-click the file to extract its contents. On Linux use the unzip command.4) Complete the instructions in the enclosed text file, README.txt5) The updates provided in this workaround are for releases R2010b, R2011a, R2011b and R2012a only.

Workaround: You need to initialize the MATLAB structure array argument as show below: Set t = CreateObject("MWComUtil.MWStruct7.17") Call t.Initialize(Array(m, n), Array("FieldName1", "FieldName2", ...)) m and n are the dimensions of the MATLAB structure array. If you don't know the dimensions ahead of time, you can specify the struct as a 1X1 array. The structure array dimensions will be automatically recalculated at runtime. FieldName1 and FieldName2 are the field names of the structure array. They need to match with the field names in the MATLAB function, and they are case sensitive.

Summary: Internal error on update diagram when multiplying by booleans in Stateflow
Description: An internal error might occur when updating block diagrams with Stateflow charts with the following properties: The chart performs a multiplication of a boolean by a built-in integer The chart's overflow mode is set to wrap (not saturate) The Stateflow debugger is enabled with breakpoints on overflow  As a result, diagrams with these properties cannot be updated or simulated. The internal error has the following text: Wide multiplication previously used for Stateflow overflow checking is no longer allowed.
Workaround: To prevent the error and produce numerically equivalent results, cast the boolean argument to uint8.For example, replace expressions such as b*foo (b is boolean) with uint8(b)*foo.

Summary: For integer and fixed point portions of a model, analysis can be incorrect or fail if the embedded hardware is not equivalent to a 32-bit target
Description: Depending on the embedded hardware that a model is targeting, a bug in Simulink Design Verifier may cause analysis to be incorrect or fail. The main impact of this bug is to change data types used in the model. This bug may also cause a slight change of rounding behavior for fixed point casts if several conditions are met.1  The main impact of this bug does not affect models configured to target embedded hardware that provides char, short, int and long of 8, 16, 32 and 32 bits. If the model is exposed to this bug, analysis may fail or return incorrect results. Floating point and boolean portions of a model are not exposed to this bug. You may use the attached script "detectModelExposure" to determine if a model is exposed to this bug. A model's embedded hardware configuration affects the model's behavior. The embedded hardware affects the automatic selection of data types for operations that involve integer or fixed point arguments. The properties of the embedded hardware are used to guide the selection of data types that will lead to a balance of the most desirable mathematical behavior and the most efficient embedded implementation. This bug in analysis temporarily alters the model's embedded hardware to a generic 32-bit embedded processor. If the model's original embedded hardware was ASIC/FPGA or if the original number of bits for char, short, int or long did not equal 8, 16, 32 and 32, then automatic selection of data types for the original model and the altered model can be different. A change in data types could cause a data type propagation error which would cause analysis to fail. The change of data type can also lead to different mathematical behavior between the original model and the altered model. Altering the mathematical behavior of the model can produce incorrect analysis. Included in the attachments are two models that demonstrate when this bug can be triggered. In the first model, "mSimulinkExample" the sum block is configured to use internal rules to determine the accumulator and output data types. In the second model "mStateflowExample", a relational operator in the Stateflow action language is used with mixed integer types. In both models, the embedded hardware configuration affects automatic data type selection and resulting mathematical behavior. Due to this bug of temporarily altering the embedded hardware, the analysis for these models is incorrect. Note 1 : Independent of the main issue of changing data types, this bug may also cause a slight change of rounding behavior for fixed point casts if all of the following criteria are true. The original embedded hardware specified that signed integer division rounds to Zero or Floor. The optimization 'Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers' is selected. The model uses fixed point types with Slopes that are not powers of two.  When all these criteria are true, some fixed-point casts may have a 1 bit rounding difference between the original model and the altered model.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: For models containing action subsystems inside enabled susbystems, behavior of generated code might differ from normal simulation behavior
Description: For models that contain If Action Subsystem or Switch Case Action Subsystem blocks within enabled susbystems, the behavior of the generated code might differ from the normal simulation behavior if any of the contained action subsystems has a disable method. Specifically, when the enabled subsystem is disabled, the disable methods of all  contained action subsystems are executed, rather than just the disable method of the active  contained action subsystem.

Summary: bitset leaks memory if the third input is of floating point type
Description: bitset  accepts an optional third input, V , for specifying the new bit values, 0  or 1 . A memory leak occurs if you pass V  as a value of type double  or single . For example, specifying V  as a double  (the default type): bitset(uint32(50),1,0);  or as a single : bitset(uint32(50),1,single(0));
Workaround: To work around this issue, specify V as a logical or integer type. For example:bitset(uint32(50), 1, false);bitset(uint32(50), 1, uint32(0));

Summary: Unexpected warning related to configuration parameter mismatch between a parent model and a referenced model
Description: If all  of the following conditions exist, then simulating the model causes incorrect diagnostics to be issued: A model has the Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Model Referencing > Model configuration mismatch  parameter set to warning  or error . The model contains a Model block. The model uses a variable-step continuous solver, and the referenced model uses a variable-step discrete solver.  The incorrect diagnostics are similar to these: Warning: Solver parameter 'Max step size' does not match between top model 'testMain' and model 'childA' referenced by block 'testMain/A'. Simulink will use the top model's setting of '0.1' Warning: Solver parameter 'Relative tolerance' does not match between top model 'testMain' and model 'childA' referenced by block 'testMain/A'. Simulink will use the top model's setting of '0.001'  ?

Summary: Headers and footers cannot occur on report title pages
Description: The Report Generator's Header Content and Footer Content stylesheet elements erroneously provide a "Title page" option. The option is erroneous because the Report Generator does not support headers and footers on title pages. This option will be removed in future releases.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: for-loop with integer or fixed-point index variable is incorrectly normalized
Description: MATLAB Coder has an optimization which normalizes loops so that they start from zero. This optimization does not guard against overflow conditions, which in certain cases can cause incorrect code to be generated.  For example, the normalization optimization converts: for i = int8(-128) : int8(126)    x = x + double(i);end To: for i = int8(0) : int8(126+128)    x = x + double(i-128);end This is incorrect because 126+128  is not representable as an int8 . This bug occurs only when an integer or fixed-point type is used for the loop index and the number of iterations cannot be represented by the type of the loop index. The bug does not occur when the type of the loop index is double, which is the default in MATLAB.
Workaround: Use an integer type which is large enough to accommodate the normalized range. In the above example, the normalized range is 0:254, so int16 is large enough to accommodate the range.An alternative workaround, which avoids the use of a larger type for the index variable, is to manually normalize the loop using an unsigned type:for i = uint8(0) : uint8(254)    x = x + (double(i) - 128);end

Summary: Wrong tooltip information for pointers when the size of char is 16bits
Description: When the size of char is 16bits, the size of pointers given in bytes by Polyspace is wrong: it is twice the real size. Example with a target where a char is 16 bits an int is 32 bits, and this code: typedef unsigned int mytype; // 32 bits so 2 bytes  mytype t;  void g(void) { mytype *p = tab;  p = &t; t = *p; }  on *p we have the tooltip: local variable 'p' (pointer to unsigned int 32, size: 32 bits): pointer is not null points to 4 bytes at offset 0 in buffer of 2 bytes, so is outside bounds  may point to variable or field of variable in: {t}  Problem: the sentence "points to 4 bytes" should be "points to 2 bytes". Please note that it is only a tooltip problem, the checks are correct (green in this example) ?

Summary: Code generation might fail when Simulink function contains MATLAB Function block
Description: Code generation might fail if all of the following conditions are true: The model has a Stateflow chart that contains a Simulink function. The Simulink function contains a reference to a MATLAB Function block in a library. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation > Report  pane, the Traceable MATLAB functions  check box is selected.
Workaround: In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation > Report pane, clear the Traceable MATLAB functions check box.

Summary: Memory leak when using sim command
Description: There is a memory leak when using the sim  command to simulate models that call MEX-functions in the Interpreted MATLAB Function block.
Workaround: Starting the simulation from the Simulink editor has no memory leak. Only starting the simulation from MATLAB using the sim command has the memory leak.

Summary: Train fails with when Java is not available
Description: Training fails when MATLAB is run with Java disabled (i.e., MATLAB is opened with the -nojvm  option).
Workaround: To work around this issue:1. Open the nntraintool file in the editor with the command:edit nntraintool2. Locate the following section of the file, and insert the four lines of code indicated as %NEW.if ~usejava('swing')  if (nargin == 1) && strcmp(command,'check') result = false;  result2 = false;  return  elseif (nargin == 1) && strcmp(command,'clear_stops') % NEW  result1 = [];  % NEW result2 = [];  % NEW return;        % NEW else  error(message('nnet:Java:NotAvailable'));  end end 3. Save the file and close the editor.

Summary: MATLAB can crash when using anonymous functions in listener callbacks if the anonymous function calls a method of an object that contains the object generating the event.
Description: If you add a listener to an object's event with code like the following: addlistener(obj.Parent,'MyProp','PostSet',@(s,e)obj.myMethod(s,e));  Then obj  contains the object (in the Parent  property) that is the source of the event for which you are listening. MATLAB can crash under these circumstances.
Workaround: Use an ordinary function or method function as the callback. For example, create the listener using:addlistener(obj.Parent,'MyProp','PostSet',@obj.myMethod);

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: For models containing action subsystems, behavior of generated code might differ from normal simulation behavior
Description: For models that contain If Action Subsystem or Switch Case Action Subsystem blocks, the behavior of generated code might differ from the normal simulation behavior. Specifically, in cases where the disable method of the action subsystem should be called once, in the generated code, it might be called twice. If invoking the disable function a second time causes further changes to the state of the model, this difference in behavior might lead to unexpected results.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation for if statements containing isempty(persistent variable) logically or-ed with an always-true condition
Description: If your code has an if  statement that contains  isempty(persistent variable)  logically or -ed with a condition that always evaluates to true , then, in some rare cases, MATLAB Coder might not compile parts of the code. In these cases, this bug results in wrong answers.
Workaround: Avoid  if conditions that have isempty(persistent variable) logically or-ed with some other condition.?

Summary: For Each Subsystem or Pack Boolean data into bitfields might introduce unused global variable
Description: The generated code might have an unused global variable if all of the following conditions are met: In the global structure, an embedded global variable, which is created by either a For Each Subsystem or the selected configuration parameter Pack Boolean data into bitfields , is not in a true global state. All references of the global variable are localized in the generated code.  A variant of this issue is the generated code has an unused empty nested structure, which causes uncompilable code.

Summary: Block dialog or mask parameter expression might cause CGIR assertion 'Size mismatch' during code generation
Description: Block dialog or mask parameter expression might cause CGIR assertion 'Size mismatch ' during code generation if all of the following conditions are met: The parameter expression contains a subexpression that needs to be reduced (loss of tunability) The subexpression is an operant of an operator whose other operant is a tunable parameter expression The operator has a matrix return type
Workaround: Remove the tunability of the parameter expression or reduce the tunable parameter expression to a single tunable parameter.?

Summary: Computed table entry background colors do not work
Description: The Report Generator's Table Entry component does not honor background colors specified as MATLAB expressions. An example of a MATLAB expression for a color is %<EXPR> , where EXPR  evaluates to a color specification of the form #rrggbb.
Workaround: You can use Report Generator logical (if-then-else) components to specify conditional background colors. For example, suppose you want the background color of a table entry specifying a test result to be green if a test passes and red if the test fails. The following pseudo code indicates a solution based on logical components:result = test();if result == passthen green entryelse red entryend

Summary: signrank does not ignore zeros in a single-sample test
Description: The signrank  function ignores ties when comparing two samples, or when comparing one sample to a specified value:  signrank(x,y)  However, signrank  incorrectly fails to ignore zeros when it is provided with a single sample and no explicit specified value for comparison: signrank(x)
Workaround: To make an explicit comparison with zero, provide a second input argument : signrank(x,0)

Summary: CODER.NULLCOPY does not "initialize" persistent variables
Description: For code like the following: persistent p;if isempty(p)   p = coder.nullcopy(zeros(3,3));   for i = 1:9       p(i) = i;   endend  MATLAB Coder incorrectly reinitializes the persistent variable every time it is used. coder.nullcopy  is not viewed as initializing the persistent variable. This bug affects all usage of MATLAB code generation including the MATLAB Function block in Simulink, MATLAB Functions in Stateflow, and the FIACCEL function in the Fixed-point Toolbox.
Workaround: Do not use coder.nullcopy on persistent variables.

Summary: System bus outputs reported incorrectly
Description: The Simulink Property Table incorrectly reports the data type, width, and dimensions of a system output signal that is a bus. It should report the data type as the name of the bus object that defines the output bus, the width as 1, and the dimensions as [1 1]. Instead, it reports other values for these properties derived from the bus object that defines the bus.

Summary: Interpolation might return incorrect results for narrowly spaced sample points
Description: interp1 , interp2 , interp3 , interpn , and griddedInterpolant  might produce incorrect results when the sample points are very narrowly spaced. For example, x = logspace(-11,-10,150);y = linspace(.8,1,150);xq = linspace(min(x),max(x),10);yq = interp1(x, y, xq,'linear');figure;plot(x,y,'-',xq,yq,'*');
Workaround: Scale the values in x and xq by numel(x). For example,yq2 = interp1(numel(x)*x,y,numel(x)*xq,'linear');figure; plot(x,y,'-',xq,yq2,'*');

Summary: The Load Flow parameters of the Three-Phase Programmable Voltage Source block are not visible
Description: The Load Flow parameters of the Three-Phase Programmable Voltage Source block are not visible in the Load Flow tab. Therefore it is impossible to setup the block for a load flow.
Workaround: To work around the issue:1. Open powerlib library. 2. Unlock the library. 3. At the MATLAB prompt, type: MV = get_param('powerlib/Electrical Sources/Three-Phase Programmable Voltage Source','MaskVisibilities'); MV{17} = 'on'; set_param('powerlib/Electrical Sources/Three-Phase Programmable Voltage Source','MaskVisibilities',MV); save_system('powerlib'); close_system('powerlib');

Summary: A long running loop can expose a memory leak when the Workspace browser is open
Description: When MATLAB code is running in a long loop with a call to drawnow  or pause , a memory leak can occur if the Workspace browser is open. If the loop runs for several hours, a noticeable amount of memory will be consumed.
Workaround: Before starting a long computation loop that uses drawnow or pause, close the Workspace browser. When MATLAB returns to the command prompt, the leaked memory will be released to the operating system.

Summary: Library block loses embedded signal object when loaded as part of model
Description: When you load a model containing an instance of a library block, if an embedded signal object is specified at the output port of that library block, the embedded signal object is lost.

Summary: parallel.gpu.CUDAKernel incorrectly rejects PTX generated by CUDA Toolkit version 4.1 or greater
Description: parallel.gpu.CUDAKernel  incorrectly rejects some PTX that is generated by nvcc  in CUDA Toolkit version 4.1 or greater with the error message "No entries were found in the PTX file" . This happens with PTX generated for GPU architectures sm_20 or greater. For example, PTX generated with the command: $ nvcc kernel.cu -ptx -arch=sm_20 causes an error when loaded by CUDAKernel : >> k = parallel.gpu.CUDAKernel('kernel.ptx','kernel.cu')Error using handleKernelArgs (line 61)No entries were found in the PTX file. Compiling for architecture 1.3 (sm_13) or earlier works as expected.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. Summary: Error when running a deployed application using Optimization Toolbox plot functions.
Description: If you use plot functions when calling an Optimization Toolbox solver in a deployed application, you can get an error. This error is due to a bug in plot function routines. The routines unnecessarily require a Java application, which deployed applications do not have.

Summary: ssc_build does not report location information for errors in Simscape files
Description: During ssc_build, the location information of errors in Simscape files is not reported; instead only the error message is reported.  For example, the following message is printed when running ssc_build on a package that contains a file with syntax error: >> ssc_build testGenerating Simulink library 'test_lib' in the current directory '/tmp' ...Failed to generate 'test_lib'Caused by:    Error using ne_buildpackage (line 32)    Error: Expression or statement is incorrect--possibly unbalanced (, {, or    [.  Instead the following message that contains the location information about the syntax error should be generated: >> ssc_build testGenerating Simulink library 'test_lib' in the current directory '/tmp' ...Failed to generate 'test_lib'Caused by:    Error using ne_buildpackage (line 32)    Error: File:    /tmp/+test/t1.ssc Line: 47    Column: 14    Expression or statement is incorrect--possibly unbalanced (, {, or [.
Workaround: Run the following command to add <MATLAB>/toolbox/physmod/common/foundation/core/m directory to your MATLAB path for current MATLAB session, or edit pathdef.m to add it to your MATLAB installation permanently. addpath([matlabroot '/toolbox/physmod/common/foundation/core/m'])

Summary: loadlibrary structure parsing error with sized vectors in structure definitions with a chained data type
Description: This structure does not parse correctly: struct foo { int a, b, c[20]; };  However this one does:   struct bar { int a, b; int c[20]; };  ?
Workaround: Modify the structure definition in your header file or create a loader MATLAB file with the mfile name loadlibrary option and fix the broken field. For this example:structs.foo.members=struct('a', 'int32', 'b', 'int32', 'error0', ' c [ 20 ]');Should be: structs.foo.members=struct('a', 'int32', 'b', 'int32', 'c', 'int32#20');?

Summary: Template makefile path macros might expand to relative paths instead of fully-qualified paths when a Windows path includes a space
Description: During code generation on a Windows system, template makefile (TMF) path macros might expand to relative paths instead of the expected fully-qualified paths if the Windows path includes a space. For example, if you generate code for the model rtwdemo_autosar_counter  with the current folder set to C:\work , the path to a folder named "stub " appears in the generated MK file as a full path: MATLAB_INCLUDES = $(MATLAB_INCLUDES);$(START_DIR)\rtwdemo_autosar_counter_autosar_rtw\stub However, if you generate code for rtwdemo_autosar_counter  with the current folder set to C:\work 2 , the path to "stub " appears in the generated MK file as a relative path: MATLAB_INCLUDES = $(MATLAB_INCLUDES);.\stub
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file, open the .zip file and look at the contents.Extract the files from the .zip file:Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ¡°Error: Access is denied¡± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode.
