Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation when function-call signals are branched and corresponding Function-Call subsystems have different handling of states upon re-enabling
Description: This bug occurs when all  of the following conditions are met:  A Function-Call Split block is used to branch a function-call signal and feed the individual branches to multiple Function-Call subsystems. At least one of above Function-Call subsystems has the Trigger block parameter States when enabling  set to off . At least one other of above Function-Call Subsystems has the Trigger block parameter States when enabling  of the  set to on . Code for the model containing these subsystems is generated using Simulink Coder or Embedded Coder.  When this bug occurs, the Initialization functions for the Function-Call subsystems described above are not invoked in the generated code, causing a difference in Simulink and code generation results.
Workaround: For all Trigger blocks within the above Function-Call subsystems, set the States when enabling parameter to be the same (either set all to on or all to off).

Summary: Block hyperlinks missing from System Design Description (SDD) report
Description: Hyperlinks to Subsystem blocks are missing in System Design Description (SDD) reports. For example, all of the block names listed in the Block Execution Order section of the report should be hyperlinks to the block parameter tables. However, only the names of blocks in the root system are hyperlinks.

Summary: If a model uses Goto and From blocks to connect blocks with unspecified rate transitions, and the destinations have triggered sample times, Simulink does not detect the unspecified rate transitions
Description: Simulink does not detect unspecified rate transitions when: The connection between the blocks is made with Goto and From blocks The destination block has a triggered sample time (for example, because it is inside a triggered subsystem)

Summary: Error from geotiffinfo when reading certain files
Description: In R2012a, the geotiffinfo  function does not properly read certain GeoTIFF files that contain an empty CTProjection  field. Instead, it issues the following error message: Error using containers.Map/subsref   The specified key is not present in this container.
Workaround: To work around this problem:Log into your computer using an account with Administrator privileges.Download the attached file, workaround_814114-12a_2012-04-13.zipOn Windows double-click the file to view its contents, then click the Extract button. On Macintosh, double-click the file to extract its contents. On Linux use the unzip command.Complete the instructions in the enclosed text file, README.txtThe updates provided in this workaround are for release R2012a only.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Functions that are called from within an expression and modify global variables might produce incorrect answers
Description: A global variable is any variable declared using the global keyword. For example:     global x;  A function that directly or indirectly writes to a global variable, and is called nested within an expression might produce incorrect answers. For example, in the following code, the function f()  modifies the global g .    ...  global g;   t = g;   y = t + f();   ...   In this example, f()  is nested inside an expression. You might get an incorrect answer where it appears that the call to f()  occurred before the assignment t=g .
Workaround: Rewrite all functions that modify globals so that they appear directly to the right of an assignment. Rewrite the example:   ...   global g;   t = g;   y = t + f();   ...into    ...   global g;   t = g;   tf = f();   y = t + tf;   ...

Summary: Additional object libraries specified in model configuration parameters are not added to the MSVC solution generated for a model
Description: When using the Simulink Coder build process, the Microsoft Visual C++ (MSVC) compiler, and an MSVC target to generate an MSVC solution file for a model, additional object library paths specified using Code Generation > Custom Code > Libraries  in the the Configuration Parameters dialog box are not correctly included in the build. The build terminates with an error message similar to the following: ### Build procedure for model: <modelname> aborted due to an error.
Workaround: To work around this bug, add the object libraries to the Additional Libraries folder in the MSVC project using the following steps:1. Create a build process 'before_make' hook function that adds object libraries to the model in the manner shown below.%CustomRTWBeforeMake.mfunction [str, status] = CustomRTWBeforeMake(modelName, dependencyObject)str =sprintf('Custom before make hook for model ''%s.''',modelName);disp(str)addLinkObjects(dependencyObject, ... 'bar.lib', 'C:\Documents\MATLAB\foo', 1000, ... true, false );status =1;2. Create the following sl_customization.m file, or add the content below to an existing sl_customization.m file. To register the specified build process hook, the sl_customization.m file must be in the MATLAB path when Simulink is opened.%sl_customization.mfunction sl_customization(cm)% Get default (factory) customizationshObj = cm.RTWBuildCustomizer;% Register build process hookshObj.addUserHook('before_make', 'CustomRTWBeforeMake(modelName, dependencyObject);');end

Summary: Using a large number of model arguments in a referenced model may cause a crash
Description: If a referenced model uses a large number of model arguments, then simulating or generating code for a parent model that references this model may cause an invalid memory access and possibly result in a crash or other undefined behavior. The maximum number of model arguments that can be safely used depends on the length of the variable names in the model workspace. Typically the safe limit is between 100 and 150 arguments. To detect whether this bug affects your model, download the attached file and run the detection script at the MATLAB command prompt: detectG813202(model);  where model  is the name of your referenced model that is using parameter arguments.
Workaround: Consider using a tunable structure parameter to contain the model arguments.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink Coder may allow code generation for a model with a virtual bus signal whose initial value is improperly specified
Description: You model may be subject to this bug under the following conditions: Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection  is set to Classic . The model contains a virtual bus signal whose dimensions correspond to a vector with the same data type for all of its elements. The above virtual bus signal is driving an Outport block of a conditionally executed subsystem, such as an Enabled Subsystem or a Triggered Subsystem. Initial output  of the above Outport block is specified as a vector with at least 2 elements, but the number of elements of the initial output is less than the number of elements in the virtual bus signal.  Because of the above inconsistency between the initial output and the virtual bus signal dimensions, Simulink Coder may produce one of the following results: Incorrect initialization code for the above virtual bus signal, with seemingly random value for the elements that are not covered by the initial value. The following error message with no code generated: Unexpected or internal error encountered in "in CGIR:dom_core/lowering/ScalarizeVcat.cpp line 920: fLength == selRegions.getLength()". Please report this to MathWorks if you can cause it to recur.
Workaround: Do one of the following:Ensure that the initial output value for the Outport block has the same size as the virtual bus signal being initialized.Use scalar initial output values for virtual bus signals to prevent the above mistake, whenever possible.Run Model Advisor check Check consistency of initialization parameters for Outport and Merge blocks to upgrade your model to use simplified initialization mode. To specify an initial value for a bus signal in simplified initialization mode, either use a scalar value or a structure value (for R2010a or later; see section Specifying Initial Conditions for Bus Signals in product documentation).

Summary: Boxplot function fails with scalar grouping variable and name-value pair input arguments.
Description: There is a bug in Statistics Toolbox 7.6 (R2011b) in the way that BOXPLOT handles a scalar valued grouping variable when using name-value pair input arguments. An error can occur when the grouping variable equals 1 or 0. Reproduction Code : % Example Datadata = randn(1,100);groups = 1;  % BOXPLOT call that results in an errorboxplot(data,groups,'plotstyle','compact');
Workaround: To workaround this issue, pass the grouping variable in a cell array to BOXPLOT.Example workaround :% Example Datadata = randn(1,100);groups = 1;% BOXPLOT call that avoids the errorboxplot(data,{groups},'plotstyle','compact');?

Summary: Compile error if multiple interrupts from an asynchronous function-call initiator drive the same function-call subsystem
Description: The build process might generate uncompilable code for a function-call subsystem if both of the following conditions are true: Multiple asynchronous function calls from a single function-call initiator (for example, an array of function-call signals) drive the same function-call subsystem. The asynchronous rates associated with the function calls are not combinable, for example, because the asynchronous rates have differing Simulink task priorities.  Under these conditions, in the generated code, the entry function of the asynchronous task does not correctly call the function-call subsystem code, and compilation of the generated code fails.
Workaround: If your design requires multiple asynchronous function calls from a single initiator to drive the same function-call subsystem, you must assign the same priority to the asynchronous rate associated with each function call.

Summary: New instances of Attribute Function block do not run
Description: If you add a new  Attribute Function block to your model from the SimEvents / Attributes library, when you compile the model, you see the following message. Race condition detected between 'untitled' and 'untitled/Attribute Function/__DMLF__SA__'. The former block initiates function calls during entity advancement, but the latter block responds to such function calls after the entity has arrived. This may cause the latter block to process the entity using outdated signal values.  This message falsely indicates the presence of a race condition in your model. The message is caused by the new Attribute Function block, which is in an unusable state. However, any existing Attribute Function blocks in your model remain unaffected.Additionally, if you copy a pre-existing Attribute Function block in your model, the copied  instance of the block becomes unusable, while the original instance is unaffected. You see the following message. Warning: Error evaluating 'CopyFcn' callback of Attribute Function block (mask) 'untitled/Attribute Function1'.Warning: No appropriate method, property, or field Name for class handle.handle.  The workaround below instructs you to delete unusable instances of the Attribute Function block, before saving the model and installing the software fix that accompanies this Bug Report. Summary: Model to code navigation might not work properly after regenerating code
Description: After generating code and the code generation report for a model once, you modified your model, and then regenerated the code and code generation report, model to code navigation might highlight the wrong elements in the report. After regeneration of the code and report, the model to code mapping is not updated in the report. This issue does not occur the first time you generate code and the code generation report for a model.
Workaround: In the code generation report, right-click on any blank space in the report and choose Reload.

Summary: save_system error when using a file extension or folder path
Description: You see an error from save_system if the first argument is a file name and not a block diagram name, i.e., if you include a file extension or folder path. For example:>> save_system('mymodel.mdl'); Error using save_system (line 38)Invalid Simulink object name: mymodel.mdl
Workaround: To avoid this error, do not include file extensions or folder paths and pass only the block diagram name to save_system. For example:>> save_system('mymodel');The model is saved in the same file from which it was loaded.  To specify a file location and/or file extension explicitly, use a second argument:>> save_system('mymodel','C:\projects\mymodel.slx');

Summary: Running destroy or getAllOutputArguments on a batch job generates an error
Description: A new programming interface for accessing clusters, jobs, and tasks was introduced in R2012a. All jobs created using the batch function use this new programming interface, so calling destroy  or getAllOutputArguments  on the resulting job object throws an error. Batch jobs that are created from a scheduler object use the old programming interface, so do not exhibit this error. The following examples show code that runs with and without the error: % Create a batch job that runs a script, then try to destroy the job.j = batch('myScript');destroy(j);No appropriate method, property, or field destroy for class parallel.job.CJSIndependentJob.% Create a batch job that runs a function, then try to get% the output arguments from it.j = batch(@myFunction, 1);out = getAllOutputArguments(j);No appropriate method, property, or field getAllOutputArguments for class parallel.job.CJSIndependentJob.% Create a batch job that runs a script using a scheduler% object in the old programming interface.s = findResource();j = batch(s, 'myScript');destroy(j); % No error thrown% Create a batch job that runs a function using a scheduler% object in the old programming interface.s = findResource();j = batch(s, @myFunction, 1);out = getAllOutputArguments(j); % No error thrown
Workaround: There are two workarounds for this issue:Use the new programming interface to interact with the job created by the batch function. Instead of calling destroy, use delete; instead of getAllOutputArguments, use fetchOutputs.Follow the installation instructions below and continue to use destroy and getAllOutputArguments in the old programming interface.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a Linux shell or in a Macintosh terminal window:unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a Linux shell or in a Macintosh terminal window: cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ¡°Error: Access is denied¡± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you might need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Summary: Multipath Rician Fading Channel block can consume excessive memory as simulation progresses.
Description: The Multipath Rician Fading Channel  block from the Channels library  in the product has logging enabled for an underlying component signal. This signal logging can result in excessive memory being used by the block, dependent on the simulation time and the step-size used for the Simulink model.
Workaround: A workaround is to turn off signal logging for the whole Simulink model using the Multipath Rician Fading Channel block.This can be achieved by clearing the Signal logging checkbox on the Data Import/Export pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog for the model.

Summary: Simulink may crash when using global data stores across a model reference hierarchy
Description: Simulink may crash when simulating, generating code or performing an update diagram on the top model, if a model meets the following requirements: The model contains Model blocks; At least one of the referenced models is using global data stores.  The crash that is reported varies, but it may be related to a sample time issue.

Summary: Parameters conflicting with UNIX command echo may not be passed to standalone executable when using run script generated by mcc.
Description: Some parameters may be interpreted by the echo  command and will not be passed into a standalone executable when using the mcc -generated run script. For example: ./run_myapp.sh /path/to/mcr -n   will result in no parameters being passed to the executable.
Workaround: Workarounds include:Passing the parameter with escaped parentheses and stripping them off using MATLAB code.For example:./run_myapp.sh /path/to/mcr \"-n\"Modifying the run_myapp.sh script to include a guard on line 32, changing the line from:token=`echo "$1" | sed 's/ /\\\\ /g'` to:token=`echo "#$1" | sed 's/ /\\\\ /g' | sed 's/#//'`

Summary: The TLC function STRING might crash MATLAB session when expanding parameter into numerical representation
Description: The TLC built-in utility function, STRING  might crash MATLAB session if all of the following conditions are met: The function is used to expand a parameter into its numerical representation. The input to the STRING  function call contains only the parameter to be expanded.

Summary: Table Column Spec and Table Entry dialog boxes can fail to appear
Description: The Table Column Spec and Table Entry dialog boxes can fail to appear when you select a Table Column Spec or Table Entry component in the Report Explorer's report outline view. This occurs if you unselect the Table Rules Between Columns  option on the parent table's dialog box.
Workaround: To eliminate all rules between columns, unselect the Table Rules Between Columns option as the last step in creating or editing a table.

Summary: Lifting scheme fails to correctly update final coefficient for odd-length inputs and certain primal lifting steps
Description: If your 1-D input signal has odd length, or your 2-D input matrix has an odd-length row or column dimension, the lifting scheme may fail to correctly update the final coefficient (primal lifting step). You observe this bug if the Laurent polynomial associated with the primal lifting step has a nonzero coefficient for a negative power of the variable z. For example: displs(liftwave('bior2.2'))  In this case: x = [1 2 1 2 1];  [A,D] = lwt(x,liftwave('bior2.2'));  produces the incorrect value for A(3) , the final approximation coefficient. ? ? ?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation for model with reusable conditionally executed subsystem whose output is unconnected or connected to a Terminator block
Description: You model may be subject to this bug if all  of the following conditions exist: The model is configured to use simplified initialization mode, i.e. Configuration Parameters > Diagnostics > Data Validity > Underspecified initialization detection  is set to Simplified . The model contains a conditionally executed subsystem whose output port is unconnected or connected to a Terminator block. The parameter Function packaging:  of the subsystem is set to Reusable function . For the Outport block corresponding to the above subsystem output port, Source of initial output value  is set to Dialog . The above Outport block is driven by an output port of a source block that whose signal memory is persistent across simulation time steps. Examples of such source blocks include, but are not limited to, conditionally executed subsystems, Stateflow, or a Model block whose referenced model contains root Outport blocks driven by blocks with such persistent outputs. The initial output value of the source port is different from that of the destination Outport block in the reusable subsystem above.  In the simplified initialization mode, the persistent source signal and the Outport block in the reusable subsystem should never share the same signal memory. This is by design so that there is no ambiguity when the two signals have different initial values. However, this is not the case in the generated code when all of the above conditions are met. Because of this defect, the initial output value of the Outport block overrides the initial output value source signal. This can lead to different numerical results if the initial value of the Outport block, rather than the initial value of the source signal, is read before the source block updates its output for the first time. The difference between simulation can code generation are illustrated in two examples below. A model containing these examples is also included in the attached file attachment_809322_8b_through_12a_2012-08-04.zip . Example 1  The source signal is an output of another conditionally executed subsystem and the source output also drives another destination block that is different from the destination Outport block. If the "other" destination block reads the input signal in question before the source subsystem executes, it will read the initial value of the source subsystem Outport in simulation but will read the initial value of the destination Outport in generated code. Example 2  Source signal is an output of a Stateflow block and the initial output of the source output is used by the Stateflow block itself to compute other signal or data values. In this case, the initial value specified for the Stateflow output is used in simulation, but the initial value of the destination Outport block is used in the generated code. .
Workaround: Download and unzip the attached file attachment_809322_8b_through_12a_2012-08-04.zip to a folder and add the folder to the MATLAB path using the addpath command. Run the script on your model, using the following syntax:result = DetectForBugReport809322('modelname')The script will compile the model to perform the analysis so you must ensure the model updates sucessfully before using the script. For a model that is unaffected by this issue, the script returns an empty array. If this issue can potentially impact your model, the script returns a structure array with the following fields:ReusableSubsystem: Full path name for a subsystem that may have a problem.UnconnectedOutputsWithIC: Vector containing indices of output ports that are unconnected and have non-empty initial output value for the above subsystem.After running the script, check whether the output ports of the conditionally executed subsystems reported in the result satisfy the conditions described in the "Bug Description" section.To address this issue, do one of the following:Eliminate unused output ports from conditionally executed subsystems.Ensure that every output ports of every reusable conditionally executed subsystem is connected to a block that is not a Terminator block (e.g. a Signal Conversion block).

Summary: Compatibility check incorrectly flags bus data type as incompatible
Description: A Simulink Code Inspector compatibility check might incorrectly flag a bus data type as incompatible when code inspection does support that data type. This problem can occur when a Simulink.Bus object defines a nested bus, and the type of one or more bus elements is of the form Bus:SUBBUS_TYPE , with no space between Bus:  and SUBBUS_TYPE .
Workaround: There are two workarounds:1. Include a space prior to the name of the SUBBUS_TYPE (e.g. specify the bus element type as Bus: SUBBUS_TYPE). 2. Create the Simulink.Bus object using the Simulink Bus Editor, which adds the space.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink generates incorrect code when a Product block has a single variable-sized input, and the block's input and output signals have differing fixed-point datatypes
Description: Simulink generates incorrect code for a model if all  of the following conditions are true: The model contains a Product block with a single input port. The signal feeding this input port is a variable-size signal. The input and output signals of the block have differing fixed-point datatypes.  To check whether this issue affects your model, run the DetectForBugReport809155.p  script. See the Workaround  section below for details.
Workaround: Download and unzip the attached Attachment_809155_20120430.zip script to a folder and add the folder to the MATLAB path using the addpath command. Run the script on your model, using the following syntax:ret = DetectForBugReport809155('modelname')For a model that is unaffected by this issue, the script returns an empty array. If this issue is detected in your model, the script returns an array of structures with the following fields:ProductBlock: The single input Product block that demonstrates the problem outlined.InputDataType: The datatype of the input signal to this Product block.OutputDataType: The datatype of the output signal from this Product block.The following workaround will convert the Product blocks that demonstrate this problem to have identical fixed-point datatypes for their input and output signals:Place a Data Type Conversion block before the Product block input (see the description above).Set the Data Type Conversion block parameter Output data type to Inherit: Inherit via back propagation to convert the signal to the same data type as the Product block output signal.

Summary: Unable to acquire from all channels with the Matrox Morphis
Description: When using the Matrox Morphis, only the first channel, CH0 corresponding to the VID_IN0 signal as described in the Morphis manual, is available. This means that only the first input is available to all Morphis devices detected by the toolbox. When using the Image Acquisition Toolbox, the Morphis should appear as multiple devices. Each device should have multiple sources depending on the DCF used to create the device. For example, when using the Morphis Dual with an NTSC camera, there should be two Morphis devices, each with sixteen sources. When using the Morhphis Quad with an S-Video camera, there should be four devices, each with eight sources. Currently each device will only have a single source regardless of the DCF used to create the videoinput object.

Summary: Warning message "initializer is too large, this may cause scaling troubles" during compilation
Description: With certain code, the Polyspace verification may generate the following warning message during the compilation phase: file.c, line xxx: warning: initializer is too large, this may cause scaling troubles. The first N constants of M will be kept and the rest will be replaced by random  This warning occurs when the software automatically truncates a very large initializer to avoid the no memory space  error during compilation. This truncation of the initializer may have some effects on results, for example, more orange checks and pointer aliasing. ? ?
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your Polyspace installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your Polyspace installation. By default this is in c:\PolySpace or in /usr/local/PolySpace on Linux systems. This folder is referred to as <POLYSPACE> in subsequent steps. PolySpace_Common and PolySpaceFor<lang>_R20xx are the two principal sub-folders of <POLYSPACE>.Close the Polyspace verification environment.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell:unzip -l <POLYSPACE>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <POLYSPACE> folder.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell: cd <POLYSPACE>unzip <.zip file>Check that binaries have appropriate ¡®read & execute¡¯ permissions.Delete the .zip file in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Reopen the Polyspace verification environment.From the Project Manager perspective, select the Configuration > All Settings tab.Select Analysis options > Polyspace inner settings > Other options.In the Value field, enter -cfe-extra-flags --no_initializer_limit.Note: This workaround applies only if there is no chance of a no memory space error occuring during the compilation phase.?

Summary: Analysis completes but does not show results, reports error Index exceeds matrix dimensions
Description: If the name of a root level Inport or Outport block includes a character that is not a letter or a space, such as "[" or ">", the following error appears in the MATLAB Command Window. Index exceeds matrix dimensions. The Simulink Design Verifier log window might show that the analysis completed normally, but it does not proceed to displaying full analysis results and the Simulink Design Verifier Results window does not appear.

Summary: Externally scoping global variables in nested functions might yield incorrect results
Description: In rare cases, using a variable as both a global variable and an externally scoped variable in a nested function might yield incorrect results.  For example, the global variable var  will not be incremented properly in the following program.function bug  global var;  function nested    foo;    bar(var);  % use of a global as an uplevel  end  var = 1;  for i=1:4     nested;  endendfunction foo  global var;  var = var + 1;  t = baz([0; 0]);  fprintf('var = %d\n', var);  % the value is incorrect hereendfunction bar(var)endfunction x = baz(b)    x = b;end
Workaround: Avoid using externally scoped global variables.  Alternatively, the jitting of function calls can be disabled by using the following feature command: feature jitallow mfunctions off.

Summary: Non-compilable code generated for complex Stateflow function calls
Description: Simulink Coder might generate non-compilable code for a model, if The model contains a complex Stateflow chart, which calls a Subsystem block. The Subsystem block is inlined. There are two sample times in the model. The Stateflow chart, which calls the Subsystem block, must be large enough such that the code of the subchart is generated into a separate C function.  The generated code might not have a declaration for a local block I/O variable, therefore it will not compile. ?
Workaround: For the affected Subsystem block, open the Subsystem block parameter dialog, click the Code Generation tab, and change Function packaging to either Function or Reusable Function.

Summary: GigE Vision adaptor loses packets on Mac OS X
Description: When acquiring from a GigE Vision camera on Mac OS X, you may encounter the error: gige: Block/frame 2 is being dropped because a lost packet is unable to be resent. The most likely cause of the lost packet is that the device-specific (source) PacketDelay is not set correctly. Also, the buffer size of the socket could not be set to 40363392. See your system administrator about using sysctl for net.inet.udp.recvspace. For example, the system administrator could try: "sysctl -w net.inet.udp.recvspace=40363392" If this is not anoption, see the troubleshooting information in the "Configuring GigE Vision Devices" section of the Image Acquisition Toolbox documentation.

Summary: Atomic, inlined subsystem might cause assertion during code generation
Description: A subsystem with Function packaging  set to Inline  might cause an error during code generation. The error includes the text:   CGIR assertion  If the subsystem is in a referenced model, this error might also occur during accelerated simulation.
Workaround: Identify the subsystem causing the error by performing a verbose build. Change the subsystem parameter Function packaging to Reusable function or Function.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Top model code is not regenerated after aperiodic trigger properties for concurrent execution are modified
Description: When you modify aperiodic trigger properties in the concurrent execution configuration for a top model, the next model build should always regenerate the top model code. However, the checksum calculation used by the build process to determine whether the top model code is up to date omits aperiodic trigger properties. Therefore, the build process might incorrectly determine that the generated code is current with respect to the top model and might fail to regenerate the top model code.
Workaround: To work around this bug, take one of the following actions:To force top model code to always be regenerated for the model, select the model option Enable TLC assertion, which is located on the Code Generation > Debug pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box. (The corresponding command-line option is TLCAssert.) This setting is saved with the model.To force top model code to be generated in a programmed build, specify the ForceTopModelBuild option to rtwbuild or slbuild.rtwbuild(model,'ForceTopModelbuild',true)slbuild(model,'StandaloneRTWTarget','ForceTopModelBuild', true)To force top model code to be generated for the duration of the current MATLAB session, disable top model incremental builds with the following command. (To reenable top model incremental builds, specify the same command with true in place of false.)slprivate('setTopModelIncrementalBuild',false);

Summary: Models using signal logging and protected models may fail to simulate
Description: When all  of the following are true, a model fails to simulate:  A protected model is referenced inside of another referenced model. There is no model with the same name as the protected model on the MATLAB path. Signal logging is turned on for the top model. The Model block that references the model that either directly or indirectly references the protected model is set up to "Log signals as specified by the referenced model".
Workaround: Disable the logging of signals for the Model block in the top model that references the model that either directly or indirectly references the protected model. Prior to R2011a, this is done by right-clicking on the Model block, selecting "Log Referenced Signals...", and unchecking "Log signals as specified by the referenced model".From R2011a on, this is done by right-clicking on the Model block, selecting "Log Referenced Signals...", setting "Logging Mode" to "Override signals", and unchecking the Model block in the "Model Hierarchy".

Summary: Models with Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine block that run fine in R2010b or earlier releases produce unexpected results in R2011b and R2012a
Description: In R2010b, the inductances for the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine block are defined in the form of [Ld, Lq]. In R2011a, the block has a new parameter, Rotor type , which makes it possible to model both round and salient rotor machines, as opposed to just salient pole rotors in R2010b. Unfortunately this parameter is set to Round  by default, causing the difference in outputs when a model saved in R2010b is opened in R2011b. The consequence of having this parameter set to Round  is that the [Ld, Lq] Inductances are not used by the model and it is the armature inductance La that is used (with a default value of 8.5 e-3). This difference in machine parameters is causing the different simulation results.  The Rotor type  parameter is set by default to Salient-pole  in R2011a. This means that the problem does not exist when you open in R2011a an old model built in R2010b. Similarly, the issue does not exist when you open in R2011b models with Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine created in R2011a.
Workaround: In R2011b, change the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine block parameter Rotor Type to Salient-pole. This causes the model to use the inductances [Ld, Lq], as specified in previous releases, and therefore produces the expected results.

Summary: xPC kernel crashes while executing a model with file scopes, dynamic file mode enabled, and autorestart disabled
Description: The xPC Target kernel can crash during application startup under the following circumstances: The model includes a Scope (xPC) block of type File configured programmatically to enable dynamic file mode. Autorestart is disabled on the Scope block.
Workaround: Workaround is to ensure to disable Dynamic File Mode for all file scopes in a model that have AutoRestart disabled.blocks = find_system(bdroot, 'autorestart', 'off', 'dynamicfilemode', 'on')cellfun(@(blks) set_param(blks, 'dynamicfilemode', 'off'), blocks)

Summary: Code generation for some loops exhausts system memory or runs forever
Description: If an indexing expression in a loop contains multiple variables, an analysis performed by the code generation software can run until it exhausts the available memory or may appear to run forever. In this situation, MATLAB?  crashes with an out of memory condition. Loops in Stateflow?  and MATLAB can cause this condition. The loop in the following example causes the out of memory condition:   function R = example(M,S,n)  coder.inline('never');  UU = 1;  R = 0;  for i=1:n     si  = UU;     ei  = UU + S(i) - 1;     A   = M(si:ei);         UU = ei + 1;     R = R + numel(A);  end  In this example, the interaction among ei,  UU , and the indexing expression M(si:ei)  causes the out of memory condition.
Workaround: To avoid this bug, upgrade to a release that includes the bug fix. If you cannot upgrade, use the following workaround:Identify the loop that causes the slowdown. Although you cannot identify the loop with certainty, you can look for: A loop that contains complex conditions such as incrementing a variable used in indexing.A variable whose value grows slowly (commonly caused by code such as x = x + 1, but also caused by incrementing and decrementing by any value or by multiplication). In the preceding example, UU and ei interact to cause UU to grow.Create a variable ZERO whose value is unknown at compile time and is zero at run time. Use one of the following methods: In Stateflow?, define a tunable parameter called ZERO that contains the value o.In MATLAB?, thread an input called ZERO from your entry-point file.Define: ZERO = n - (n - 1) - 1;where n is a variable whose value depends on an external input.Rewrite the assignment that causes the variable to grow:               UU = ei + 1 + ZERO;             Because the code generation software does not know the value of ZERO, it stops the analysis that causes the out of memory condition.

Summary: Undefined behavior when accelerating models that have mismatched case modelName_acc.mexext filenames on the MATLAB path
Description: Undefined behavior when accelerating models that have mismatched case modelName _acc.mexext  filenames on the MATLAB path. If the following is true: You set the Accelerator mode and click the Start  button in the Simulink editor window to run a model. A MEX-file is on the MATLAB path whose only difference from modelName _acc.mexext  is the case.  MATLAB becomes unresponsive. You might see the error message: The model is still running, do you want to stop it and close the model?
Workaround: As a workaround, do one of the following:Rename the model.Instead of clicking the Start button on the Simulink editor window, use the sim command at the MATLAB prompt, for example sim('modelName'). This command causes an error that contains the location of the other MEX-file. You can either remove that folder from the MATLAB path, or rename that MEX-file.

Summary: Possible SegV when number of mask parameters of a masked block are changed after simulating a model with inline parameters on
Description: Models that contain masked blocks may cause a segmentation violation if all the following conditions are met: In the Configuration Parameters > Optimization  dialog for the model, you check the Inline Parameters  option. You simulate the model. After simulation, you change the number of mask parameters of a masked block.
Workaround: If you want to change the number of mask parameters of a masked block, uncheck the Inline Parameters option in the Configuration Parameters dialog of the model before simulating the model.

The software reports an error similar to the following: Data type 'sldemo_bus_arrays_busobject' for block 'mChild/Constant', output port 1 in model 'mChild' is invalid. Custom data types are not supported when logging signals in referenced models; data types must be builtin, or fixed-point ?

Summary: The expression of Ibase in the Three-Phase V-I Measurement block reference page is not correct
Description: The expression of Ibase in the Three-Phase V-I Measurement block reference page is incorrect. The documentation should read:           Pn     sqrt(2)Ibase = ---- * ---------          Vn     sqrt(3)

Summary: Generated code for integer division operations may include redundant division-by-zero protection.
Description: This bug occurs only if the following conditions are true: - The Model Configuration Parameters Optimization pane "Remove code that protects against division by zero exceptions" parameter is not selected - You are generating code for integer or fixed-point division operations If both these conditions are true, the generated code might contain multiple copies of the division by zero protection code. This extra code has a small effect on the code size and run-time performance, but does not affect numerical correctness. This bug does not affect floating-point divisions.
Workaround: For ERT targets, disabling division protection by selecting the "Remove code that protects against division by zero exceptions" checkbox on the Model Configuration Parameters Optimization pane eliminates the problem.Using unsigned divisions that do not need rounding code (simplest, floor, or zero rounding modes) reduces the likelihood of multiple protections.?

Summary: MATLAB crashes when generating HDL Traceability Report
Description: HDL code generation from Simulink may cause MATLAB to crash if both the following conditions are met:  HDL Traceability Report generation is enabled   The model contains a Stateflow chart with a junction whose only exit transition is unconditional
Workaround: Disable generation of the HDL Traceability Report, or modify the Stateflow chart so that it does not contain a junction with a single unconditional exit transition.

Summary: Paragraph changes not reflected in Report Explorer's Outline pane
Description: The Report Explorer's Outline view of a Paragraph component does not reflect changes to the component's text that result from editing the component in the Report Explorer's Dialog view of the component.

Summary: Serial communication for an External mode target application might fail if the target implementation of rtIOStreamSend is nonblocking
Description: External mode target applications that use serial to communicate with a Simulink model on the host system might fail if the target-side rtIOStreamSend  function is implemented as nonblocking -- that is, implemented to return potentially before all requested data has been sent, rather than blocking until all requested data has been sent. After rtIOStreamSend  is invoked on the target, the Simulink model might disconnect from the target or wait indefinitely for data to be received from the target.
Workaround: To work around this bug, implement the target rtIOStreamSend function so that it blocks (does not return) until all requested data has been sent. For reference, here is the rtIOStreamSend interface:int rtIOStreamSend(int streamID, const void * src, size_t size, size_t * sizeSent)The input argument size specifies the amount of data to send over the specified communication stream. The implementation should set output argument sizeSent to the value of the size argument. Return the value zero to indicate success, or the value RTIOSTREAM_ERROR (defined in rtiostream.h) to indicate an error.

Summary: MATLAB terminates abruptly when running SimEvents model containing Event Filter block
Description: When you run a SimEvents model that contains an Event Filter block, MATLAB might terminate abruptly if the number of events received at the input signal of the Event Filter block exceeds the value for Maximum events per block  or Maximum events per model  that you set in the SimEvents pane of the Model Configuration Parameters dialog box.
Workaround: When you simulate your model, if the value of Maximum events per block or Maximum events per model is exceeded, this usually indicates that a livelock condition exists in the model. Most commonly, a livelock condition occurs if you configure a feedback loop in the model that causes a block to return to the same state infinitely at the same time instant.Alternatively, if your simulation exceeds the value of Maximum events per block or Maximum events per model, the value of this parameter might be too small for the requirements of your model. If this is the case, in the SimEvents pane of the Model Configuration Parameters dialog box, you can increase the value.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier reports compatibility error Translation failed for subsystem analysis
Description: If you attempt to perform Simulink Design Verifier analysis on a subsystem, Simulink Design Verifier reports that the subsystem is incompatible if the subsystem contains a Stateflow chart with an output signal that resolves to a Simulink signal object. Simulink Design Verifier shows a compatibility error that looks like the following: Translation failed: Global Simulink.Signal object 'XYZ' implies a global Data Store Memory block for the reference model 'Enabled', so the property 'Complexity' may not be set to 'auto' This error message is related to a hidden DataStoreMemory block. This block is inserted to support a global data store corresponding to the Simulink.Signal object 'XYZ' in the global workspace

Summary: A referenced model that uses a structure parameter as a model argument may generate uncompilable code
Description: A referenced model generates code that does not compile if all  of the following are true: The referenced model is using model arguments, and one of the arguments is a MATLAB structure parameter. That structure parameter is used by a block in the referenced model and that block lives in a subsystem. The subsystem has set the Function packaging  parameter to Function .  In this case, you may see an error similar to this: midLevel.c(29) : error C2440: '=' : cannot convert from 'const struct_8Kw1LQkjLGsuD3QJCvmFAH *' to 'struct_8Kw1LQkjLGsuD3QJCvmFAH'  This error happens in either of these situations: When generating code using Simulink Coder When simulating this model hierarchy, if the model is referenced in Accelerator mode
Workaround: The error does not happen when simulating the referenced model in Normal mode. To avoid the bug for code generation, make sure that no subsystems that contain a block using a structure parameter has its Function packaging paramter set to Function.

Description: If you insert a digital I/O block that uses the 8255 chip into a model and change the channel parameter in the mask without first updating the diagram, you might see the error 'Attempt to reference field of non-structure array'.
Workaround: After inserting a digital input or output block into a model, perform an update system (^D) before changing the channel parameter for the block.

Summary: Option -k-limiting produces wrong results for pointer aliases
Description: When the value of the -k-limiting  option corresponds to pointer depth, Polyspace generates a red ASRT check instead of a green check. With the following code, if you specify the option -k-limiting 1 , Polyspace generates a red ASRT check for the **a  pointer. #include <assert.h> #include <stdlib.h>  void incr1(int *p) { *p = *p + 1; }void incr(int *p) { incr1(p); }  int main(void) { volatile int random; int b=1, c=2, **a, *p;  a = (int**)malloc(sizeof(int*) * 2); a[0] = &b; a[1] = &c; if (random) { int before = *(a[1]); int *p = a[1]; incr(p); assert(before + 1 == *(a[1])); //Should be green ASRT }  return 0; }  ?
Workaround: Avoid using the -k-limiting option.

Summary: Programmatically setting SimState loggedStates can cause MATLAB to crash for models that contain root-level atomic subsystems whose contents have no blocks with logged states
Description: Programmatically setting SimState loggedStates can cause MATLAB to crash for models that contain root-level atomic subsystems whose contents have no blocks with logged states. For example, if xFinal  is a MATLAB object of type Simulink.SimState.ModelSimState , the following command is expected to save logged states of blocks within this object: xFinal.loggedStates(n).values = myValues; However, if you specify this command for a model that contains an atomic subsystem whose children blocks do not have any logged states, MATLAB might crash. This behavior can occur when Simulink tries to set the saved logged state (in a SimState object of the given model) of a child block whose execution order is behind that of the atomic subsystem.
Workaround: As a workaround, try setting the block priority of the given atomic subsystem to one that is lower than that of any of the other blocks that contain SimState logged states.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when opening a MATLAB Coder configuration object created without using the coder.config function.
Description: If you create a MATLAB Coder configuration object as shown in the following example, MATLAB crashes: cfg = coder.EmbeddedCodeConfig;   open cfg;
Workaround: Create the configuration object using the coder.config function. For example, to create a configuration object for a library build:cfg = coder.config('lib');open cfg; Alternatively, explicitly set the HardwareImplementation property of the MATLAB Coder configuration object.

In a Mapping Toolbox map axes, the frame is quadrangular for cylindrical, pseudocyilindrical, conic, and all other non-azimuthal map projections.
Workaround: To work around this problem you can:Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to below as <MATLAB>.Make a back-up copy of the file <MATLAB>/toolbox/map/mapproj/private/trimPolygonToQuadrangle.mExit MATLAB.Edit the file <MATLAB>/toolbox/map/mapproj/private/trimPolygonToQuadrangle.m. (You may have to change file permissions first.)In subfunction mergeEndToEndPairs, replace the expression onEdge = (x(first2) == xlimit(1)) | (x(first2) == xlimit(2)) with onEdge = abs(x(first2) - xlimit(1)) < tolSnap | abs(x(first2) - xlimit(2)) < tolSnapRestart MATLAB and run rehash toolbox at the command line.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Simulation and code generation results may differ for an If block when using unary minus operator with non-floating point data type input signal(s)
Description: Using a unary minus operator in the If or Elseif expression of the If block may lead to different simulation and code generation results when the block has non-floating point data type input signal(s). The If block does not handle overflow conditions that could occur when the unary minus operation is performed on a non-floating point data type. As an example, consider the application of the unary minus operator on the value -128  for the data type int8 . In simulation, all values in the expression are converted to double  before the unary minus is applied. This allows the generic expression evaluation engine to work with all data types, but the Overflow condition is not detected. Consequently, the expression -e  where e = -128  evaluates to 128 . In generated code, the unary minus operation is performed according to the data type. Assuming two's complement  system, the binary representation of -128  is, 10000000 . The two's complement negation of this number is obtained by complementing all bits and add 1, which is 01111111  + 1 = 10000000 . Consequently, the expression -e  where e = -128   still evaluates to -128 , which is not the same as the simulation result above. We now give a few examples to demonstrate the difference described above. All examples assume the input data type is int8 . Example 1  The If block has one input signal. The If expression is -u1 > 0 . The input value for the current time step is u1 = -128 .  In simulation, -u1 > 0  evaluates to 128 > 0 , which is true . In generated code, -u1 > 0  evaluates to -128 > 0 , which is false . Example 2  The If block has one input signal. A variable w  exists in the main workspace and w = -128 . The If expression is u1 > -w . The input value for the current time step is u1 = 1 .  In simulation, u1 > -w  evaluates to 1 > 128 , which is false . In generated code, u1 > -w  evaluates to 1 > -128 , which is true . Example 3  The If block has two input signals. The If expression is u1 > -u2 . The input values for the current time step are u1 = 1  and u2 = -128 , respectively.  In simulation, u1 > -u2  evaluates to 1 > 128, which is false . In generated code, u1 > -u2  evaluates 1 > -128 , which is true .
Workaround: Do one of the following:Use the Unary Minus block on the input signal(s) of the If block instead of using the unary minus operator in the If or ElseIf expression of the If block. The Unary Minus block supports integer overflow correctly and will help you avoid this issue.For signed integer data types, design your system so that the input signals never reach the most negative value of the data type. For example, if the input data type is int8, ensure that the signal values are between -127 and 127.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when nargin or nargout is called with an empty function handle
Description: MATLAB crashes when nargin  or nargout  is called with an empty function handle, for example nargin(function_handle.empty) .
Workaround: Check whether the function handle is empty before calling nargin/nargout,if ~isempty(x) n = nargin(x)else  error('empty function handle') end

Summary: Polyspace Tool Operational Requirements (TOR) test fails for MISRA-C rule 2.2
Description: To perform the Tool Operational Requirements (TOR) test, you have to run the command .\qualkitdo_polyspace_tcpr.bat  from the folder qualkits\do\polyspace\r2012a\tests  (with Linux, the equivalent command is./qualkitdo_polyspace_tcpr.sh ). However, the TOR test fails with the following message: ============================================================================ 2.2: fully source code shall only use /* ... */ style comments. ======= misrac/misra-2-2.c --> FAILED Following reference is not found in results : 27 : MISRA-C WARNING : rule 2.2 (required) violated. 37 : MISRA-C WARNING : rule 2.2 (required) violated. See also Execution-Folder/misrac/misra-2-2/verification.log ============================================================================  This error occurs because of a slight change in the verification output in R2012a: the location of the MISRA-C violation is on a different line. The same error is also generated by the IEC Certification Kit.  ?
Workaround: Do the following:Unzip the attachment.Update the file misra-2-2.c, which is found in the misrac folder.Rerun the TOR test command.The full path for the misrac folder is as follows:DO Qualification Kit: Install_folder\qualkits\iec\polyspace\r2012a\tests\misracIEC Certification Kit: Install_folder\qualkits\do\polyspace\r2012a\tests\misrac

Description: Moving or copying a table can create spurious table components. This occurs when you move or copy a table from one location in a report to another.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. Summary: Element order within an AUTOSAR internal behavior ARXML file might change with each code generation run
Description: The order of certain elements within a generated AUTOSAR internal behavior ARXML file might change with each code generation run. The resulting ARXML code is still AUTOSAR-compliant, but when compared with the ARXML output of a previous code generation run, might unnecessarily clutter the comparison with non-functional differences. The AUTOSAR elements for which the reordering might occur are DATA-READ-ACCESS, DATA-WRITE-ACCESS, and SHARED-CALPRM-ACCESS-REF. For example, suppose an initial model build generates the following ARXML code: <DATA-READ-ACCESS><VARIABLE-ACCESS>    <SHORT-NAME>First </SHORT-NAME>    ...</VARIABLE-ACCESS> <VARIABLE-ACCESS>    <SHORT-NAME>Second </SHORT-NAME>    ...</VARIABLE-ACCESS></DATA-READ-ACCESS> The next model build might generate ARXML code in which the first and second elements are swapped: <DATA-READ-ACCESS><VARIABLE-ACCESS>    <SHORT-NAME>Second </SHORT-NAME>    ...</VARIABLE-ACCESS> <VARIABLE-ACCESS>    <SHORT-NAME>First </SHORT-NAME>    ...</VARIABLE-ACCESS></DATA-READ-ACCESS> A comparison of the two ARXML files will indicate differences that do not reflect a meaningful change to the AUTOSAR configuration.

Summary: Lookup Table blocks might produce unnecessary signal overflow warning or error messages at run time
Description: When ALL of the following conditions are true, the n-D Lookup Table and Interpolation Using Prelookup blocks might produce an unnecessary signal overflow warning or error message at run time: Input data type and output data type are fixed-point. Table data  data type and Intermediate results  data type are the same and don't have any bias. Table data  data type is unsigned. The model configuration parameter, Detect overflow  (Configuration Parameters >  Diagnostics > Data Validity > Signals), is set to "warning" or "error".

Summary: Possible loss of values while loading models containing links to certain masked blocks in libraries created before R2011b
Description: Since R2011b, block parameters of a linked block can be changed only if they are promoted on the library block. Links to blocks that are not of type Subsystem, Model, or S-Function discard the value of the non-promoted parameters when you load a model containing these linked blocks.
Workaround: The Upgrade Advisor in Simulink checks and updates masked blocks to use promoted parameters. The check searches libraries created before R2011b for masked blocks that should be updated to use promoted parameters and updates such masked blocks. This check excludes blocks of type Subsystem, Model, and S-Function.The attachment contains a library (mg796684_Lib) with a constant block masked and a model (mg796684) having a link to this constant block. Observe that the value of constant block will be set to '3' on opening the model in releases before R2011b. Observe that the value of constant block gets set to '1' on opening the model in releases after R2011b. From R2011b, since the parameter 'value' of the constant block was not promoted its value gets discarded while opening the model. This can be fixed by promoting the parameter 'value' of the constant block in the library (mg796684_Lib). Running the Upgrade Advisor on library can help detect and update such masked blocks.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink models with CAN Pack and Unpack blocks generate incorrect code
Description: Models with CAN Pack and Unpack blocks can generate incorrect code when you specify large integer values in the Min and Max fields. For example if you specify 4294967295 (intmax('uint32')), the block rounds the number off to 4294967000 in the generated code.
Workaround: If you are using Vehicle Network Toolbox version R2012b or earlier, contact Mathworks Technical Support for a workaround.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Linked Stateflow charts with multiple instances might produce incorrect results during simulation
Description: A linked library chart might produce incorrect results during simulation if you: Instantiate the chart multiple times in a model Enable the property Export Chart Level Functions (Make Global) , but the chart has no top-level functions to export
Workaround: Disable the property Export chart level functions (Make global) for library charts that do not have top-level functions. There is no negative impact on simulation because this property applies only when you have functions at the top level. The attached script findProblemCharts.m finds all charts that have this property enabled, but no functions to export. The attached zip file also contains a model main1.mdl which exhibits this problem.

Summary: linearize might error on model that references models with different solver types
Description: The linearize  command errors when the following conditions hold: The main model references other models in normal mode. At least one of the referenced models uses a different solver type from the main model. For example, the main model uses a variable-step solver and one of the referenced models uses a fixed-step solver. You invoke the linearize  command using an operating point object as an input argument.  For example,  op = operpoint(mdl); sys = linearize(mdl,getlinio(mdl),op); Under these conditions, linearize  returns an error similar to the following: Index exceeds matrix dimensions. Error inB:\matlab\toolbox\shared\slcontrollib\+slcontrollib\+mdlcfg\ParameterManager.p>ParameterManager.restoreParameters(line 323) set_param(activeConfig, prop, obj.OrigConfigSetParameters(ct).(prop)); Error inB:\matlab\toolbox\shared\slcontrollib\+slcontrollib\+mdlcfg\ParameterManager.p>ParameterManager.restoreModels(line 86) restoreParameters(obj); Error in linearize>LocalLinearizeModel (line 412) ModelParameterMgr.restoreModels; Error in linearize (line 328) [sys,InspectorData,iostruct] = LocalLinearizeModel(ModelParameterMgr,LinData,iostructfcn);
Workaround: As a workaround, use the same solver type (variable-step or fixed-step) for the main model and all models referenced in normal mode. To do so, for each model:In the Simulation menu, select Model Configuration Parameters to open the Configuration Parameters dialog box for the model.In the Solver options area of the dialog box, select the desired solver type from the Type drop-down list. Click OK.

Summary: Erroneous value updates in struct arrays in C++ Shared Libraries generated by MATLAB Compiler
Description: A MATLAB function is compiled to a C++ Shared Library that returns a struct array with all fields initialized to the same value. A different value is then assigned to one of the fields in the struct array, causing all fields to be updated erroneously to the same value. For example, a MATLAB function (InitStruct.m ) creates a struct array with three fields and initializes each field to 1, as shown below: function result = InitStruct()  result = struct('field1',1,'field2',1,'field3',1);InitStruct.m  is compiled into a C++ Shared Library (mytest.lib ). In the C++ main() , InitStruct()  is called to get the struct array. The first field is assigned as 0  (zero), as follows: mwArray cible;  InitStruct(1, cible);  //print out the value for each field   std::cout << "field1 = " << cible("field1",1) << std::endl;  std::cout << "field2 = " << cible("field2",1) << std::endl;  std::cout << "field3 = " << cible("field3",1) std::endl;  std::cout << "field1 is changed to 0" << std::endl;  cible("field1",1) = 0;    //print out the value for each field again   std::cout << "field1 = " << cible("field2",1) << std::endl;  std::cout << "field2 = " << cible("field2",1) << std::endl;  std::cout << "field3 = " << cible("field3",1) << std::endl; If the C++ code and mytest.lib are compiled into an executable and run, the following output is generated: field1 = 1field2 = 1field3 = 1field1 is changed to 0filed1 = 0field2 = 0filed3 = 0 Notice that even though the value of only the first field (field1 ) was changed, the other two fields (field2  and field3 )were updated as well.
Workaround: Instead of assigning the field to a primitive data type  directly, wrap it in an mwArray before the assignment, as shown below: cible("field1",1) = mwArray(0);Alternatively, avoid initializing the fields to the same value in your MATLAB function, if possible.

Summary: load function does not recognize carriage returns ('\r') as line-endings in -ascii mode
Description: When importing data from a text file, the load  function interprets carriage return characters ('\r' ) as white-space, rather than as line-endings. For files with lines that are terminated exclusively by carriage returns, the load  function imports them as a single row. For example, given the following file ('\r'  added for clarity): 1, 2, 3\r 4, 5, 6\r load  imports these data as: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] These data should have been imported as: [1, 2, 3;4, 5, 6] Note that this only affects files with lines terminated by carriage returns. Files with lines terminated by line feeds ('\n' ) or by carriage returns and line feeds ('\r\n' ) will load correctly, since line feeds are still seen as line-endings.
Workaround: Use an equivalent MATLAB function, such as dlmread or importdata, to load your data. You must have write access to all the code being used to load the data files. Your code should then work in R2012a, as well as in previous versions.For example, instead of:data = load('myDataFile.txt', '-ascii');use:data = dlmread('myDataFile.txt');

Summary: The project in CCSv3.3 IDE fails to compile while attempting Model Block Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL) simulation via TCP/IP on C6747 EVM
Description: When you configure a Model block for TCP/IP-based PIL on C6747 EVM and click "Play" on the top level model, CCS v3.3 IDE generates a Build Log error "Don't know how to build C:\Program..\c6747_i2c.cmd". This error appears when the IDE attempts to build the archive library project for the referenced model. The error occurs because the archiver cannot compile a linker command file. The linker command file should only be included when linking the final executable.
Workaround: Download the attached files according to your release:- R2010b : attachment_794241_10b_2012-05-23.zip- R2011a and R2011b : attachment_794241_11a_11b_2012-05-23.zip- R2012a: attachment_794241_12a_2012-05-23.zipExtract the zip file into a writable folder, such as C:\Users\username\DownloadsIn MATLAB set Current Folder to the location where you extracted the zip file. This folder will have two files: patch_installer.m and patch_794241.zip.At MATLAB command prompt, enter: patch_installer('patch_794241.zip', 'install');Follow the instructions displayed on the MATLAB command line.

Summary: Code generation fails for Simulink models with Boolean signals that have specified minimum or maximum values.
Description: Code generation for a model with a source block that produces a Boolean signal might produce an "Assertion failed" error message if you specify minimum and/or maximum values for the signal.
Workaround: Do not specify minimum and/or maximum values for signals of Boolean type.

Summary: Code generation fails with % and Japanese character in Stateflow? comment or MATLAB Function block comment
Description: A %  with other Japanese characters in a Stateflow? comment or a MATLAB function block comment might cause code generation to produce a stack trace and abort.

Summary: marcumq loses precision when the output values are close to 0
Description: When the output values of marcumq  are close to 0, there is a loss of precision. The difference is usually less than 1e-15.

Summary: Generated code for referenced model might fail to compile or might crash when running the generated executable
Description: The generated code for a referenced model might fail to compile or might crash when running the generated executable, if all of the following conditions are true: The model is a referenced model. One of the model's root inports is a function call inport with an asynchronous sample time. The referenced model contains a Stateflow chart with at least one graphical function and the chart is directly or indirectly connected to the asynchronous root function call inport.  A crash might happen when running a simulation of the top model with the referenced model using Accelerator  mode, or when running the generated executable for the top model as a standalone application.
Workaround: Modify your model such that at least one of the conditions listed in the Bug Description section is not true.

Summary: Performance degradation converting large cell arrays to .NET System.String arrays
Description: While calling a .NET method that takes a System.String array, significant performance decrease might be observed when passing a cell array with more than a few thousand elements.
Workaround: Create a System.String array of the required size in MATLAB. Then use a for-loop to iterate through the .NET array and populate it. Pass the .NET array to the method.The following example populates the .NET array:netstr = NET.createArray('System.String', 10000); for i = 1:10000   netstr(i) = 'hello'; end

Summary: Callback actions on Stateflow annotations sometimes cause crashes
Description: Stateflow might become unstable if after you trigger a callback from an annotation, the callback action takes focus away from the Stateflow Editor and then you close the editor without focusing on a Stateflow chart. The symptom is a stack trace containing the line: failed assertion in .\utils\object.cpp at line 2853: object[id]!=NULL

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code generation fails for MATLAB Function blocks that access arrays of buses via global variables
Description: Code generation from a MATLAB Function block or a MATLAB function in a Stateflow chart fails or produces incorrect code under the following conditions: An array of buses is declared as global memory (bound to a Data Store Memory block) in the MATLAB function The array of buses is accessed in the MATLAB function  The error occurs during code generation even if the model simulates without error. Several different error messages are possible, depending on how the MATLAB Function block accesses or indexes into the array of buses: "Index expression out of bounds. Attempted to access element 2. The valid range is 1-1." "Code generation failed T::isa((const CG::Type*)aType)" A size mismatch error if trying to assign the array of buses to one of the block¡¯s outputs Other errors are possible depending on the method of access  If your MATLAB Function block accesses only the first element of the array, it is possible that no error messages display during code generation. However, running the generated code can result in a segmentation violation (seg-v).
Workaround: To work around this issue, pass the array of buses into the MATLAB function as an input, and pass it back as an output. Do not declare the bus as a global in the MATLAB function code. If using Stateflow, add the array of buses as a data item to the parent chart using Model Explorer, with its scope set to Data Store Memory. Then you can also access the array of buses outside the chart using Data Store Read blocks.

Summary: Simulink Scope with Time Range set to auto causes an error if simulation Stop Time is also set to empty
Description: Create a new model and add a Scope block. Set the simulation Stop time  to empty. Save the model and close it. Try to reopen the model. The model does not open and you get the following error: "One or more output arguments not assigned during call to evalin."     In a similar manner, if you create a new model, set the Stop time  to empty, drag a scope and double click to open it, you will get a similar error.
Workaround: Set simulation Stop time to a valid value. If you already saved the model with Stop time set to empty, do the following:Load the model using load_system().Use set_param(modelName, 'StopTime', aValue) to set the Stop time to a valid value.Use open_system() to open the model.

Summary: Connect on icdevice() crashes MATLAB
Description: Customers connecting to instruments using NISCOPE/NIFGEN may observe crashing MATLAB upon attempting to connect to the instrument. The issue is caused by an incorrect VISA path specified in Instrument Control Toolbox. When MATLAB tries to load the VISA from the incorrect location, it crashes due to a segmentation fault.

Summary: Text can overflow into adjacent table cells in PDF reports
Description: Text can overflow into adjacent table cells in PDF reports if both  of these conditions apply: The text contains no spaces. The text includes some nonalphabetic characters, such as a numeral or an apostrophe.
Workaround: The Report Generator currently hyphenates words, if necessary, to fit them in a PDF table cell. If you want the Report Generator to wrap a word without resorting to hyphenation, you can insert a zero-width space, char(8203), at the point where you want the word to wrap. For example, the following function inserts a zero-width space after every character in a cell array of strings. This will cause the strings to wrap without hyphenation even if a cell is only one character wide.function oca = wrapCells(ica)  oca = cellfun(@insertZWSpaces, ica, 'UniformOutput', false);    function os = insertZWSpaces(is)      os = blanks(2*length(is));      os(1:2:end) = is;      os(2:2:end) = char(8203);    endend

Summary: Using a To File block with very large buses causes long simulation startup delay
Description: Using a To File block to log a bus with a large number (thousands or tens of thousands) of signals can delay the start of simulation for a very long time (up to many hours).

Summary: Incorrect current folder after opening a Simulink Project with startup shortcuts that set the current folder
Description: If you configure startup shortcuts to set the current folder, they are sometimes ignored by the Simulink Project Tool.A race condition can occur when using startup shortcuts that set the current folder, because there is a project setting on by default that sets the current folder to the project root. When there is a conflict between these settings, you might not see the current folder you expected. Startup shortcuts that cd into a folder can be overridden by the check box at the project node "Change current folder to project root on start up".The check box setting is on by default and incorrectly overrides the shortcut, unless the startup script takes a long time to run. The current folder will be the project root, unless the startup script is time-consuming, then the current folder will be the folder specified in the startup script. Two different results are possible due to a race condition in multithreaded code. The last code to run sets the working folder.If you have configured a startup shortcut to set the current folder, your setting should take precedence. In R2011b, the behavior is not always correct.
Workaround: To ensure the startup shortcuts can set the current folder, clear the check box "Change current folder to project root on start up" at the Project node in the Simulink Project Tool.

Summary: hdf5info returns wrong size if dataset extents exceed 2GB
Description: hdf5info  will return the wrong extent sizes of a dataset if the extents exceed 2^32. This will occur on the 32-bit and 64-bit Windows systems as well as 32-bit Macintosh and 32-bit Linux systems.
Workaround: hdf5info is deprecated as of R2011a. Use h5info instead.

Description: In PDF reports, a snapshot of a system or subsystem is not scaled to fit into the cell that it is supposed to occupy in a system summary table.
